posts.alt_text:
Gentle Monster just dropped VEGGIE, a collection of “folding eyewear” inspired by stems and organic shapes found in nature.

The collection combines natural vegetable-like shapes with a sleek, industrial design across ten compact foldable frames with subtle, playful temple details.

Each piece comes with a leather case that doubles as a bag charm or a key chain accessory.

posts.caption:
Following the success of their 2025 collaboration, @timberland and @jacquemus reunite to reimagine Timberland’s iconic 3-Eye Lug Boat Shoe. This time, the collaboration introduces a bold new colour: Lagoon Blue, which was also featured in Jacquemus’ SS27 collection.
The shoe transforms Timberland’s traditional boat shoe into a contemporary silhouette, blending #Timberland’s craftsmanship with #Jacquemus’ playful approach through distinctive details such as custom metal charms and aglets.
Through bold colour, quality craftsmanship and Jacquemus’ signature details, this collaboration offers a fresh interpretation of Timberland’s iconic boat shoe. For those looking to add a fresh and playful touch to their summer style, this is the perfect finishing piece.
Available from 17 July at jacquemus.com, timberland.com, and selected Jacquemus and Timberland® stores worldwide.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @timberland / @jacquemus
posts.alt_text:
On 16 July, @bawwlcf presented its Graduation Show at London College of Fashion, where 43 graduating students showcased collections that expressed their personal identities through critical design, technical skill, and storytelling.

From a collection inspired by the quiet landscapes of a coastal hometown to a contemporary reinterpretation of a 20th-century literary work, each designer transformed personal narratives and perspectives into thoughtful, innovative fashion.

This year’s show stood out not only for the strength of its concepts, but also for the craftsmanship and attention to detail demonstrated in each collection.

Congratulations to the Class of 2026. A2Z looks forward to seeing you become the next generation of fashion designers and shape the future of fashion.

Words by Julie Jeong
Edited by Josephine Jiang

Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

📷 @a2z_fashionmag 

#UAL #LCF #Womenswear #GraduationShow

posts.caption:
Gap just gave us a reason to fall back in love with denim.
Meet The Hailey Jean — a brand-new, limited-edition capsule from Gap, created in collaboration with model and beauty entrepreneur Hailey Bieber. Launching today, July 16th, the collection is a love letter to the effortless, low-maintenance denim style of the late ‘90s — the era Hailey says shaped her personal style from the start.
The capsule includes two silhouettes co-designed with Hailey herself: the Extra Baggy Jean, based on a vintage men’s Gap fit she’s loved for years, and the ‘90s Low-Rise Loose Jean — both available in three washes each. Every pair is made from 100% cotton rigid denim, designed to soften and mold to your body over time, just like a real vintage find. Each pair features custom “1996” hardware and back-patch detailing, a nod to Hailey’s birth year, along with her signature printed inside the pocket lining.
The campaign itself leans all the way into that ‘90s mood: shot by legendary photographer Mario Sorrenti, styled by Alastair McKimm, and set to The Cranberries’ “Linger” in an accompanying film directed by Charlie Di Placido.
Priced at $89, “The Hailey Jean” drops today at 12pm ET on Gap.com and in select Gap stores across the US, Canada, UK, France, and Japan — so if oversized, nostalgic denim is calling your name, you’ll want to move fast on this one. 👖✨
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @haileybieber , @gap
#Gap #HaileyBieber #Collaboration #Jeans #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
With the launch of COCO MADEMOISELLE Crush Absolu, @chanel.beauty announced that @GracieAbrams, CHANEL’s House Ambassador, has been named the muse of the new fragrance.
Thomas du Pré de Saint Maur, Head of Creative Resources Fragrance & Beauty at CHANEL, shared his thoughts on Gracie’s appointment: “Gracie naturally embodies the spirit of CHANEL. Like Gabrielle Chanel once did, she asserts herself without artifice, driven by a fierce sense of freedom and an instinctive elegance.”
Gabrielle Chanel began her journey in a cabaret in Moulins during the Belle Époque. Today, Gracie Abrams is defining a new generation through her voice on music festival stages around the world. Though their eras and stages may differ, both women share the same spirit of freedom and the courage to create their own paths. In this sense, Gracie carries on the symbolic legacy of Gabrielle Chanel as a modern muse.
More than simply the face of a fragrance, Gracie embodies the essence of COCO MADEMOISELLE Crush Absolu, bringing its spirit of freedom, boldness, and absolute elegance to life through a distinctly modern perspective.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now
📷 @chanel.beauty
#GracieAbrams #CHANEL #CocoMademoiselle
#CHANELFragrance #CrushAbsolu

posts.caption:
@sofiacoppola has long understood the rich texture of adolescence. @importantflowers, the publishing imprint she established with @mack_publishing, extends that eye across film, photography, art and fashion, and its latest title feels at home. ⁠
⁠
Here, AnOther can exclusively preview American Teen, a 240-page book bringing together @josephszabophotos’s renowned and previously unseen photographs of his students on Long Island during the 1970s and 1980s, with new texts by commissioning editor Coppola and Sonic Youth’s @kimletgordon.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, find out more about Joseph Szabo's work 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Joseph Szabo, from American Teen (Important Flowers, 2026). Courtesy of the artist and @mack_publishing
posts.alt_text:
When she was 19, @charlottegainsbourg “wasn’t well. I was a mess”. Her famous father, Serge Gainsbourg – France’s beloved chanteur-provocateur – invited her to move back in with him and recuperate at his home, 5 bis rue de Verneuil in Paris. “Everything was ready for me to step back into my childhood house with him and try to pull the pieces of my life together,” she recalls. “Then he died so suddenly.”⁠
⁠
For some 30 years, Charlotte kept her father’s house preserved as he’d left it – a “private mausoleum” where she could visit and grieve, away from the cemetery and the curious gaze and unwanted attentions of the general public. Then, in 2023, the house became a museum. But before Maison Gainsbourg opened its doors as a heritage site, Charlotte used her Hasselblad camera to document the place. ⁠
⁠
Now, these evocative pictures are on display in her debut photography exhibition, 5 Bis, curated with the help of @ysl creative director @anthonyvaccarello. The show, running as part of this year’s world-renowned @rencontresarles festival, is held in The Cloister Gallery – a sequestered space entered via a doorway on one of the medieval town’s narrow, picturesque streets. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, @ohnoitsemilyd speaks with Gainsbourg about immortalising her father’s Parisian home as she remembered it from her youth 📲 ⁠
⁠
📸 5 Bis by #CharlotteGainsbourg⁠
#SergeGainsbourg

posts.caption:
At @luma_arles, @thisispattismith and @soundwalkcollective bring a decade-long collaboration into sharper focus. ⁠
⁠
Correspondences, their first major European exhibition, moves through field recordings, film, poetry, drawing and archival material to explore ecology, migration, spiritual transmission and the memories held in sound. Across images of endangered landscapes, lost species and human-made disaster, Smith’s language responds to Stephan Crasneanscki and Simone Merli’s sonic worlds, while figures including Mary Magdalene and a Siberian shaman offer other modes of communication. ⁠
⁠
“I was also always happy to be a writer, where I don’t have to depend on anybody. I just write and it’s all coming from myself. Now I have both; I have brothers,” she says of Crasneanscki and sound artist Merli, who joined Soundwalk in 2008. “We’re equally loyal collaborators; we’re of one mind.”⁠
⁠
Approaching 80, Smith reflects on a creative partnership that continues to open new territory for her writing and for the audience alike. Read @ananda_pellerin’s interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Patti Smith & Stephan Crasneanscki: Correspondences. © Victor&Simon - Grégoire d’Ablon⁠

posts.caption:
With a voice as distinctive as ever, @jorjasmith_ returns with her third album, What Are The Odds. ⁠
⁠
Set to release in August, the 12-track album, produced by P2J and featuring Wizkid, spans genres – from garage to funk, soul and blues. “It’s about me and my relationships,” says Smith, cadenced by a Brummie lilt, “which, to be fair, is what I’m always writing about.” From thought to fruition, What Are The Odds has been several years underway, first imagined when working with P2J on her 2023 track Little Things, but actualised in just seven months. She’s excited to speak about it: “I only ever really want to talk about music.” ⁠
⁠
Ahead of the album’s release, @rose_dodd asks Smith 20 quickfire questions on her earliest memories of making music, star signs and life in the public eye. Link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Lead image: Photography by Ivor Lawson-Adamah

posts.caption:
It is not hard to imagine the pull Larry Stanton had on the men at Fire Island Pines.⁠
⁠
Best known for his pretty-boy portraits and pencil drawings, Stanton is revisited in Larry Stanton: Friends + Lovers, a new exhibition opening soon at @vstrscenter, through his photographs and films. “I’ve always thought of Larry’s photographs and drawings as two chapters of the same story. The camera allowed him to collect encounters. He photographed people he met in the streets [which] often became the starting point for his portraits,” co-curator @fabio_cherstich says. “The drawings are never simple translations of photographs. They’re acts of remembrance. Larry wasn’t interested in documentary realism […] he was searching for the emotional truth of a face.”⁠
⁠
Perhaps more so than his drawings, Stanton’s photographs have a conversational register. The washed-out images record not just the subject but the conditions of exchange. Stanton’s subjects are seduced by him, and he by them. ⁠
⁠
@w.lliam speaks with the show's curators Cherstich and @michaeljbullock to explore the exhibition at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Larry Stanton: Friends + Lovers. Photography by #LarryStanton, 1975. @larry_stanton_art

posts.caption:
Strength isn’t always loud. Sometimes, it’s quiet curiosity, unwavering kindness, and the courage to keep moving forward. Harper’s Bazaar India’s June-July cover star, Saffron Vadher (@saffron), reflects on resilience, identity, fashion, and the people who shape us in a conversation that’s as moving as it is inspiring. “I’ve always believed that life’s best moments are the ones you share. It’s never really been about where I am or what I’m doing. It’s about who I am doing it with,” she says.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Jhanvi Duggal (@jhanvii.23)
Photographer: Vivek Vadoliya (@vivekvad); Agency, East Co (@east__co)
Stylist: Ramona Tabita (@ramonatabita)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Hair and Make-up Artist: Aurelia Liansberg (@aurelia.liansberg)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Photo Assistant: Ryan Rivers (@ryanriversstudio)
Styling Assistant: Ludovica Toscano (@_ludovicatoscano)
Production: 1718 Production (@1718production)
Saffron is wearing Haryma necklace in yellow gold, topazes, onyx, garnets, emeralds, and diamonds by Cartier (@cartier); Outfit, yellow embroidered long dress, Iris leather gloves, Balenciaga (@balenciaga), paired with satin and rhinestone pumps by Manolo Blahnik x Balenciagia collection (@manoloblahnik)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #TheNowIssue
posts.alt_text:
Sonali Bendre (@iamsonalibendre), in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved), reflects on the evolving portrayal of women in cinema, why authenticity has become one of the rarest qualities today, and what it really means to be an ‘it girl’. She also speaks about the authors who shaped her worldview and how social media has changed the conversation around beauty, not just for women, but for men too.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full conversation.

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Make-up Artist: Itika Chugh (@fashionmakeupbyitikachugh)
Hair Artist: Umang Artist (@umang.artist), at Anima Creatives (@animacreatives)
Style Assistant: Grace Soni (@grace__soni_)
Maye Musk is wearing the Ethereal top with an abstract coral reef–inspired belt with sculptural organic textures, by Richa Khemka (@richakhemkalabel)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #MayeMusk

posts.caption:
Wishing the ever-graceful Katrina Kaif (@katrinakaif) a very happy birthday. From stealing hearts in ‘Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara’ and ‘Welcome’ to delivering unforgettable performances in songs like ‘Kamli’ and ‘Dhunki’, Kaif has spent years proving why she remains one of Bollywood’s ultimate it-girls. Beyond cinema, she has built an impressive legacy as an entrepreneur. As we look back at our October 2018 cover star, we celebrate a woman whose charm, ambition, and enduring star power continue to inspire millions.
📸: Errikos Andreou
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia
posts.alt_text:
Sonali Bendre (@iamsonalibendre), in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved), reflects on the evolving portrayal of women in cinema, why authenticity has become one of the rarest qualities today, and what it really means to be an ‘it girl’. She also speaks about the authors who shaped her worldview and how social media has changed the conversation around beauty, not just for women, but for men too.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full conversation.

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
Christopher Nolan has built a career on films like ‘Inception’, ‘Interstellar’, ‘Dunkirk’, and ‘Oppenheimer’, where ambitious ideas and cinematic scale have often taken centre stage. With ‘The Odyssey’, he brings that same grandeur to Homer’s epic, but this time, the emotional core takes precedence. Beneath the spectacle is a moving story of love, family, loss, and the longing to return home.
Tap the link in bio to read more.
✍️: Sagarika Choudhary (@sagarikasc)
📸: Burson PR and IMDb
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
From #TheGreatGatsby’s Jordan Baker to Diana, Princess of Wales, #ElizabethDebicki has inhabited some of popular culture’s most recognisable figures.
In our July cover story, the award-winning actor reflects on finding the humanity behind iconic characters and why every role begins with a blank slate. “But personally, I’m interested in trying to shed that and create as much open space to imagine, as you would with a character that’s entirely your own creation.”
Discover the full interview at the link in our bio.
The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
Debicki wears @Cartier throughout.
Words @tessdevivie
Photography @justinridler
Dp @alicenaish
Styling @jessicasteuarthoyler
Hair @______renyaxydis______
Makeup @victoriabaron
Styling assistant @breepritch.Stylist
On Set Production @aprh_productions
Tailor @sarah__woodall
Production Director @rebeccamooreproductions
Art Director @_cathryn.s.z
Digital Director @ariellekatos
posts.alt_text:
For #ElizabethDebicki, coming home to Melbourne is always a gift. As our July cover star, the award-winning actor reflects on family, finding meaning in the stories she tells, and why following instinct has become the guiding force of her career.

“As an Australian, we have this unspoken pact that you do not get to hate planes, flights or turbulence if you want to go back home and see the people you love.”

The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
Debicki wears @Cartier throughout. 

Words @tessdevivie
Photography @justinridler
Dp @alicenaish

posts.caption:
Elizabeth Debicki has become one of the most compelling actors of her generation. From Melbourne to Hollywood, our July cover star reflects on inhabiting iconic women, choosing stories with purpose, and why she’s only just getting started.
“My vetting process when I read a script is asking myself, if someone called me up and said you have to go on set next week, how excited am I about doing that?”
The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
#ElizabethDebicki wears Panthère de @Cartier necklace in white gold, onyx, set with four emeralds and 1,478 brilliant-cut diamonds, and Panthère de Cartier ring in white gold, set with four emeralds, onyx and 298 brilliant-cut diamonds. @toni_maticevski dress, bodysuit and skirt.

posts.caption:
It’s only days before @gracieabrams’ third studio album will be released, but the star has already reaffirmed her cool-girl status as the ambassador of @chanelofficial Coco Mademoiselle Crush Absolu. “I think the one [personality trait] we share is curiosity – a genuine, sincere, unrelenting curiosity,” says Abrams on her connection to Gabrielle Chanel. “That sense of adventure is so linked to her curiosity, which I really admire in people. It was exciting to meld those traits together for this campaign.”
Ahead of the Gracie Abrams’ debut short film for @chanel.beauty this August, find more on the gourmand fragrance through the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Phia is back in the spotlight, but this time for unwelcome reasons.
Last week, the buzzy fashion-tech startup found itself at the centre of a Bloomberg investigation that revealed it was taking credit for sales it didn’t actually drive via a tactic called “cookie stuffing.” The story quickly sparked chatter online, in large part because of Phia’s already high profile, a product of not only its celebrity investors, but also its co-founder Phoebe Gates, daughter of Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates, who launched the business in April 2025 alongside Stanford classmate Sophia Kianni.
Phia told Bloomberg it fixed the issue as soon as they were notified and declined to comment further when contacted by BoF.
BoF's Marc Bain unpacks the bigger picture behind Phia's latest headlines. Read more #linkinbio
✍️ @marcbain_
📷 @phiaco
posts.alt_text:
In 2016, the global retail landscape was dominated by successful direct-to-consumer disruptors like @Everlane, @Glossier, @Allbirds and @OutdoorVoices. Backed by hundreds of millions of dollars in venture capital, they prioritised rapid sales growth and hyper-aggressive social media customer acquisition over immediate profitability.

In this episode, senior news and features editor Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) joins senior correspondent Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) to explore why the once-dominant DTC formula ultimately unravelled — and how a quieter, lesser-scrutinised class of brands, such as @ShopDôen, @HillHouse and @Staud.Clothing, built more durable businesses by taking a different path.

🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
📷 @hillhouse

posts.caption:
Romain Spitzer, a 20-year veteran of LVMH’s fragrance division, will join Kering as CEO of Bottega Veneta, effective September 1.
Spitzer takes the reins of Bottega Veneta following a six-month search for a new leader, after previous chief executive Leo Rongone was named group CEO of Moncler Group in January.
Spitzer got his start as a beauty executive working at Guerlain, Jean-Paul Gaultier and Yves Saint Laurent Parfums. He joined LVMH’s beauty division in 2006, working on the group’s flagship Parfums Christain Dior before being named president of LVMH Fragrance Brands, where he oversaw a portfolio of fragrance businesses including Givenchy, Kenzo and Acqua di Parma as well as more recent acquisitions like Maison Francis Kurkdjian and Officine Universelle Buly.
Read more on the appointment #linkinbio
✍️ @r.o.bertwilliams

posts.caption:
After years of seasonal events, store openings and marketing activations “out east,” fashion’s activity in the Hamptons may have actually hit a peak.
“There’s just so much noise [in the Hamptons], to cut through would be near impossible,” said Erin Saluti, the founder and creative director of handbag brand Eittem.
This year, brands like Tuckernuck and Roller Rabbit are eyeing a new crop of coastal hot spots like Nantucket and the Jersey Shore to target well-to-do vacationers, seeking less crowded markets where they can stand out more easily.
BoF's Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) explores why fashion’s summer map is being redrawn. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @dairyboy, @rhode, @tuckernuck

posts.caption:
Richemont’s first-quarter sales jumped 20 percent with strong growth across all regions, particularly the U.S., as the Swiss luxury group continues to gain ground on rivals.
Sales at constant exchange rates for the three months to the end of June reached €6.33 billion with strong growth in jewellery and an acceleration in watches. Analysts had been expecting a 10 percent increase, according to HSBC.
Read more on the group’s growth drivers #linkinbio
✍️ @EricSylvers
📷 @Cartier

posts.caption:
In May, James Whitner's nearly 20-year-old business, The Whitaker Group, opened its newest flagship: a 23,000-square-foot space for its A Ma Maniére retail banner featuring two floors of retail, five hotel rooms, a restaurant with a bar, an art gallery and a rooftop lounge. For the location, it chose Edgewood Avenue in Atlanta, a strip historically connected to nightlife and Black culture in the city.
More than ventures into experiential shopping and hospitality, The Whitaker Group is guided by investment in areas which directly address Black buying power that’s traditionally been overlooked.
BoF's Lei Takanashi (@leitakanashi) speaks with Whitner about the long game behind his retail network — and why he's betting on ownership, community and overlooked markets to redefine luxury retail. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @thewhitakergrp

posts.alt_text:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind. 

Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
This Sunday, @afaseleccion and @sefutbol will face off in the World Cup Final, and one team will take home the coveted golden trophy along with a custom crafted @louisvuitton trunk that safely encases the winning prize. Some New Yorkers were able to get a closer glimpse at the trophy today at the French maison’s Fifth Avenue flagship store before it heads to the pitch this weekend.
Created in the storied workshops of Asnières-sur-Seine, near Paris, the luxe trunk is covered by the signature Louis Vuitton Monogram canvas, showcasing two front panels adorned with a golden “V” – for “Victory” and “Vuitton” – painted to pay homage to the trophy’s colors.
📸: BFA, courtesy Louis Vuitton
🎥: CNN
posts.alt_text:
How do you define glamour? Like the ever-elusive concept of personal style, glamour is often intangible, innate, even aura-based. 

Enter 25-year-old Norwegian soccer player and Manchester City star @erling. Days after being knocked out of the World Cup quarter-finals by England, Haaland and his partner Isabel Haugseng Johansen dusted themselves off and headed to the Mediterranean for the @dolcegabbana Alta Sartoria show in Sicily on Tuesday. 

Haaland, who has already caught the world's attention with his sprawling collection of Hermès bags and relatable love for a tacky trinket, was dressed to the nines. 

He clashed his cream satin pants with a white double-breasted blazer, dark sunshades, a gold frog-shaped brooch and navy woven leather loafers. Most notable was his thin satin-like scarf whipped eccentrically over one shoulder, like a Milanese gentleman oozing sprezzatura. 

Not since David Beckham have we seen such pizzazz and flare in soccer fashion. 

Read more at the link in our bio. 

📸: Antonio De Masi/Courtesy Dolce & Gabbana

posts.caption:
Sandra, a 43-year-old media lawyer based in Los Angeles, had been toying with the idea of breast augmentation since her 20s, but was always put off by the pain of the procedure, the long recovery time and the idea of having "this kind of foreign sac in your body."
So when Sandra, who wanted to protect her identity for privacy reasons, heard about a new injectable body contouring product last summer, she was intrigued. No synthetic fillers, no implanted sacs, no downtime.
"You hit a certain age and you decide, I'm going to do something for myself at this point," she said. The product she found — alloClae, pronounced allo-clay like the earthy sculpting material — is quick to administer. Nicknamed the lunchtime boob-job, it often takes less than an hour to inject and doesn't need general anaesthesia or a hospital setting. Plus, the aftercare instructions, Sandra said, were "shockingly easy." The fact that alloClae uses fat harvested from donated dead bodies, or cadavers as they're referred to in the scientific community, didn't really pose an issue. "I was fine with that," she said.
A growing number of people in the US appear to feel the same. Tiger Aesthetics, the company that manufactures alloClae, said more than 2,000 patients have been injected with it since May 2025. It's a buzzy topic on Reddit's popular plastic surgery page, as potential patients ask questions, seek reviews and swap doctor recommendations.
But for every plastic surgery clinic calling the injectable a "next-generation" advancement, there's an equal number of headlines branding it "zombie filler" or "corpse injections."
If there is a boundary line in the pursuit of the perfect look, are we finally butting up against it? What does it mean if society widely embraces injecting cadaver fat in the service of beauty? Could alloClae become as common as Botox?
Tap the link in bio to read more.
📸 Photo illustrations by Connie Chen/CNN/berkpixels/fStop/Getty Images

Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

posts.caption:
When Madonna and Jean-Michel Basquiat met in 1982, neither had reached the extraordinary fame that would later define them.
Their relationship unfolded between late-night clubs, artist lofts, gallery openings and the creative chaos of a city that was reinventing culture in real time. Surrounded by figures like Andy Warhol, Keith Haring and Kenny Scharf, they belonged to a generation that blurred the boundaries between art, fashion and music.
Madonna would later recall waking up in the middle of the night to find Basquiat already painting, completely consumed by his work. His relentless creativity left a lasting impression on her, while Basquiat reportedly admired Madonna’s ambition and believed she was destined for greatness long before the rest of the world did.
Their romance lasted only a few months, ending as their careers accelerated in different directions. After the breakup, Basquiat publicly asked Madonna to return the paintings he had given her and painted over them in black. Behind his extraordinary talent, however, he was also struggling with severe heroin addiction and the pressures of sudden fame. In 1988, Basquiat died from a heroin overdose at just twenty-seven years old.
Reflecting on his death years later, Madonna admitted she wasn’t surprised by the news. “He was so fragile,” she said, explaining that despite his brilliance, there was always a vulnerability about him that made his tragic fate feel heartbreakingly inevitable.
Looking back, their relationship feels almost symbolic. It captured a brief moment when two future legends crossed paths before history gave them their crowns, a reminder that some of the greatest cultural icons once shared the same uncertain beginning.
posts.alt_text:
Yves Saint Laurent didn’t just shape fashion, he also carefully shaped the way it would be remembered.

Away from the runway, the designer spent decades photographing the people, places and moments that surrounded his creative universe. From intimate portraits of friends and muses to quiet snapshots of Marrakech, Paris and his travels, his photographs reveal the same sensitivity that defined his couture. They weren’t created as fashion campaigns, but as personal memories, fragments of a life lived through beauty, art and observation.

That relationship with photography became just as important as the clothes themselves. Saint Laurent understood that fashion doesn’t exist in isolation; it lives through the images that preserve it. His own photographs sit alongside iconic portraits made by some of the twentieth century’s greatest photographers, including Irving Penn, Andy Warhol, Annie Leibovitz, Helmut Newton and Richard Avedon, illustrating how photography became inseparable from his legacy.

More than four decades later, these images feel less like documentation and more like a visual diary. Together they reveal not only one of fashion’s greatest designers, but the world that inspired him, fascinated him and ultimately became part of fashion history.

Credits

Exhibition: Yves Saint Laurent and Photography
Venue: International Center of Photography (ICP), New York
Featuring works by Yves Saint Laurent, Irving Penn, Richard Avedon, Helmut Newton, Annie Leibovitz, Andy Warhol, Sarah Moon and many other photographers from the Yves Saint Laurent archive.

posts.caption:
Some editorials are built around clothes. The best ones are built around an idea.
„The Weight of Others“ explores the invisible pressure of other people’s judgment and the quiet ways it shapes how we see ourselves. Instead of portraying this feeling literally, it transforms ordinary moments into emotionally charged scenes, revealing how external expectations can turn everyday life into a constant performance.
An overflowing office becomes a metaphor for impossible expectations. A bathroom, usually the most private room in a home, becomes a place of self-observation. Eating dinner or buying an ice cream no longer feels spontaneous but strangely performative, as though every action is being watched. The editorial captures a feeling most people recognize but rarely see visualized: the exhausting awareness of existing through someone else’s eyes.
Bold flash photography, exaggerated compositions, and vibrant colours blur the line between reality and fiction. Every frame feels slightly surreal, reflecting how anxiety and social pressure distort even the most familiar environments. Rather than documenting everyday life, the images reconstruct it through emotion, making invisible feelings tangible.
What makes The Weight of Others so compelling is its refusal to separate fashion from storytelling. The garments become part of a larger narrative rather than the destination itself, proving that fashion imagery can communicate ideas just as powerfully as it showcases design.
At a time when many editorials prioritize aesthetics alone, The Weight of Others reminds us that the most memorable fashion stories are the ones that leave you with something to think about, not just something to look at.

posts.caption:
The most revolutionary piece of couture was never a dress. It was a shoe.
Beginning in the 1920s, André Perugia transformed footwear from a functional accessory into an artistic discipline. Rejecting the conventional silhouettes of his time, he experimented with sculptural heels, impossible proportions and architectural forms that challenged the very idea of what a shoe could be. His designs weren’t created to complete an outfit—they became the outfit.
Perugia collaborated with some of the greatest couturiers of the twentieth century, including Paul Poiret, Elsa Schiaparelli and Cristóbal Balenciaga, while creating shoes for cultural icons such as Josephine Baker and Marlene Dietrich. He approached footwear with the same creative ambition that fashion reserved for haute couture, earning him the reputation as the world’s first true shoe couturier.
Nearly every luxury shoe designer working today owes something to the path Perugia created. Long before footwear became a statement piece or a collector’s object, he proved that a shoe could carry as much emotion, craftsmanship and artistic vision as any couture gown.
Modern shoe design didn’t begin with logos or red soles. It began with André Perugia.

posts.caption:
At first glance, Iván Floro’s paintings feel familiar. A face, a gesture, a memory, a moment. But the longer you look, the stranger they become.
Born in Mataró near Barcelona, Floro paints with a style that sits somewhere between Impressionism and contemporary realism. His canvases are filled with fragments of everyday life, childhood memories, desire, intimacy and countercultural references, all filtered through loose brushstrokes that feel both spontaneous and deeply intentional.
What makes his work so compelling is its ability to exist between opposites. Seriousness and irony, beauty and discomfort, nostalgia and modernity. His subjects often seem caught in a moment of emotional suspension, revealing just enough to draw you in while keeping their true story out of reach.
Floro never followed a traditional academic path. Instead, he taught himself oil painting by studying the old masters, later developing a visual language entirely his own. That combination of classical technique and contemporary subject matter gives his work a rare quality: it feels timeless without feeling nostalgic.
Perhaps that’s why his paintings stay with you. The longer you look, the less they feel like images and the more they feel like memories you can’t quite place.

posts.caption:
In an industry obsessed with minimalism, Dora Abodi reminds us that fashion can still tell stories.
The designer builds entire worlds inspired by Transylvanian folklore, medieval history and mythology. Cats, bats, forgotten aristocrats and mysterious creatures don’t simply appear as references, they become part of a universe that unfolds from one collection to the next.
What makes Abodi’s work particularly remarkable is that many of these mythical characters first exist as her own paintings and illustrations before being transformed into garments. Dresses become canvases, with hand-painted scenes, fantasy creatures and dreamlike narratives turning couture into wearable art rather than simply clothing.
Her aesthetic sits somewhere between gothic fantasy, baroque futurism and high fashion, yet it never feels like costume. Every silhouette contributes to a much larger mythology, one she has spent more than a decade developing through fashion, ceramics, illustrations and sculpture. Celebrities including Lady Gaga, Cate Blanchett, Taylor Swift, Kylie Jenner and Bad Bunny have all stepped into her imagined universe.
In a moment when so much fashion feels driven by algorithms and repetition, Dora Abodi offers something increasingly rare: proof that imagination is still one of fashion’s most powerful forms of luxury.

posts.caption:
Iconic photographer @nangoldinstudio is returning to the UK with her first institutional exhibition in over two decades.⁠
⁠
Opening at London's Hayward Gallery within the Southbank Centre, the exhibition premieres a new film, You Never Did Anything Wrong, Part II, continuing Goldin's shift away from documenting human intimacy towards the lives of animals and the natural world.⁠
⁠
Featuring a score by Mica Levi, the work combines found 16mm footage with damaged film soundtracks to create a meditation on extinction, memory and the fragile relationship between humans and other species. Part I, dedicated to Sulala Animal Rescue in Gaza, will also be shown.⁠
⁠
The exhibition opens later this year - with tickets going on sale to Southbank Centre Members on Wednesday 15 July 2026 and to the general public on Friday 17 July 2026.⁠
⁠
#nangoldin #photographer

posts.caption:
Pardon my French, but in couture’s endless game of who did it first?, let’s talk about who actually did it first. That would be Ritu Beri.
A protégée of embroidery maestro François Lesage, she became the first Indian designer invited as a guest designer on the Haute Couture calendar. She is, and always will be, THAT girl. Very First Lady of the Universe-coded.
On 17 July 1999, she presented her debut couture collection (Fall 1999/2000) at the Indian Embassy in Paris, a venue that would go on to become the backdrop for several of her shows. Behind the runway sat the now-famous Padmapani mural from the Ajanta caves. Twenty-seven years later, the same mural has the girls (grown men) fighting. Foreshadowing? Perhaps. Mrs. Beri has always possessed a curious kind of clairvoyance, the ability to spot the future of Indian fashion and arrive there before anyone else even knew the destination.
The funds were scarce. Even the models had no shoes, only feet dyed in blood-red alta. But this was an era when fashion fairy tales still had a little magic left in them. Hope and audacity could still outrun reality. Unknown designers could still assemble guest lists that included Suzy Menkes, who would go on to rave about Beri in the International Herald Tribune. Those bare red feet became symbols of sensuality. The saris and dresses were hypnotic. The audience was captivated.
Until then, the idea of India in fashion had largely been interpreted through European designers and legacy maisons. This was one of the first moments an Indian designer seized control of the lens through which the Indian story would be told. So now, while everyone wants to be the blueprint, we’re forgetting who first paved the road from Delhi to Paris. Whose ateliers cracked open the couture calendar and made space for the generations of Indian designers who followed.
In an exclusive tête-à-tête with DS, Miss ma’am Beri reflects on the realities of being first, the costs, the courage, and the satisfaction of making history.
Images: Ritu Beri, Spring 2000 Haute Couture.
posts.alt_text: Alia in this unreleased (? ☕️👀) Ekaya x Ashdeen sari, with its exquisite chinoiserie motifs I must say, immediately triggers a very specific kind of fashion déjà vu. Your mind goes straight to Madhuri Madz Dixit in that lush, deep purple sari from Didi Tera Devar Deewana. A lewk so deeply embedded in public consciousness that it becomes a reference with or without trying (let’s be honest). Been seeing a lot of DTDD references popping up lately, especially with bridesmaids. Just saying haan.

posts.caption:
Taking a military helicopter to a third grader’s golf lesson? It’s giving little prince behavior.
A new MS Now report claims Secret Service agents are “fed up” with JD Vance’s excessive personal use of government resources and tensions are especially high after he requested a military helicopter fly his nine-year-old son to a golf lesson last week. The trip was ultimately scrapped, not because anyone objected to it, but because of bad weather…and apparently this wasn’t a one-off.
The Vances have reportedly used military helicopters for multiple personal trips, including house hunting for their growing family, with Usha expecting their fourth child this month. Defense Department budget estimates put helicopter operating costs up to $24,600 per hour and naturally, taxpayers are picking up the tab.
Anonymous Secret Service sources told MS Now the VPs’ requests are not only unprecedented but frequently come at the last minute, throwing agents’ schedules into chaos and forcing rushed security planning. “They change everything,” one source said. “They don’t stick to their schedules, and that costs shit-tons of taxpayer money.”
According to the report, agents have become so frustrated with the situation that they’ve even made unofficial merch taking jabs at Vance using his Secret Service code name, “Bobcat.” Coins and stickers with phrases like “Bobcat OTR Survivors Club” and “Advance. OTR. Repeat.,” are allegedly circulating as an inside joke.
In a statement to MS Now, the vice president’s office said, “The Vances are grateful to the men and women of the U.S. Secret Service who serve our country with distinction.”
“While protecting a Vice President with a large policy portfolio and a young and growing family presents a unique challenge, agents of the Secret Service do so with excellence every day.”
#news #secretservice #threads #reels #dc
posts.alt_text:
Summer is coming to an end, and a group of California prep-school seniors are about to have a very bad year. #FXPartner

FX has released a new trailer for ‘The Shards,’ Executive Producer Ryan Murphy’s adaptation of Bret Easton Ellis’s cult novel—a sun-drenched story of privilege, paranoia, obsession, and creeping dread set against the backdrop of 1981 Los Angeles.

The early ’80s fashion alone has our attention, but with a cast that includes Igby Rigney, Kaia Gerber, Homer Gere, Hayes Warner and Graham Campbell, we’re seated.

The Shards premieres August 5 on FX and Hulu.

#theshards #breteastonellis #ryanmurphy #tv

posts.caption:
A parasitic outbreak of cyclosporiasis, a gastrointestinal illness, is reportedly affecting thousands of Americans across 31 states, causing diarrhea, nausea, dehydration, and, in some cases, hospitalizations. So far, no deaths have been reported.
Michigan has been hit especially hard, with more than 2,800 reported cases. Health officials say lettuce and other salad greens are a potential culprit, and federal and state investigators are looking into whether Taco Bell is a source of the outbreak.
This comes a year after the Trump administration gutted funding for state and local health departments and scaled back a CDC program that tracks foodborne illnesses 🙃
#dump #salad #food #news #threads

posts.caption:
Last week, Louis Vuitton won a lawsuit against Molly Tea, a China-based bubble tea brand, for trademark infringement after the tea brand’s floral logo was found to be too similar to a mark used in Louis Vuitton’s signature monogram print. The tea company was ordered to pay the French fashion giant the equivalent of $1.5m within 10 days of the ruling. However, the backlash against the ruling has afforded Molly Tea with a huge wave of support, drawing customers to storefronts in a show of solidarity, and sparking fierce criticism of LVMH online,
LVMH’s grievance with Molly Tea had to do with their use of a logo that looks similar to Louis Vuitton’s four-petal flower which was developed in the 1890s. While the logo might resemble Louis Vuitton’s signature pattern, netizens are pointing out the design’s resemblance to Chinese ancestral artworks and, such as the bao xiang hua motif, and Buddhist symbols which pre-date Louis Vuitton by thousands of years. One RedNote user described the court’s ruling as “commercial monopolization of a cultural element.”
Nevertheless, Louis Vuitton has a litigious reputation in China, having filed nearly 60 trademark infringement cases in the country this year, according to the South China Morning Post, including a recent lawsuit against China’s trademark regulator.
Molly Tea reportedly attempted to acquire trademarks for their four-petal flower logo 17 times since 2022 but faced rejections due to LV’s trademark ownership of the design. Citing these rejected applications, the Suzhou court handling Louis Vuitton’s lawsuit said that Molly Tea had “significant malicious intent” in continuing to use the logo.
LVMH’s battle with Molly Tea highlights Europe’s blind spots as it relates to Asian markets; What a brand may see as a cut-and-dry legal issue, China might see as a larger issue of cultural appropriation. Do historical designs like the four-petal flower belong to tradition or to trademark owners? Should brands even have the power to trademark ubiquitous ancient symbols in the first place?

posts.caption:
Has Haute Couture Fashion Week ever been so copacetic as Fall ‘26? It seems that the designers have reached a point of consensus, so that each show was in harmony with the next.
Of course, The Jonatthieu Anderblazy of it all is hard to ignore. Though the similarities between Dior and Chanel this week became a point of conversation, each was elegant and whimsical, and offered up titillating nods to their house’s heritages; Chanel’s ultra blown-up tweed in look 47 and Dior’s ‘Arizona Coat’ in look 29, for example.
Other little (sometimes very little) details showed up numerous times through the week. Anyone notice the little tiny flower trend? In contrast to the bulging, decadent flowers we are accustomed to seeing on runways, this week’s florals were wild, pastoral, and lively. In the case of Schiaparelli, who opened the week, they were actually alive. One look used over ten thousand flowers and required almost three thousand hours of work.
Designers also leaned into  molded bodices in a myriad of eye-catching ways. Schiaparelli molded a bodice in glazed silicone which Daniel Roseberry described as an “augmented version of reality.” The effect was so powerful that the dress flew straight off the runway and onto Zendaya, who was in London for the world premiere of ‘The Odyssey.’ Ashi Studio showed a handful of bodices, many of which seemed, frankly, like fodder for 2026 cinema press tours. And Duran Latink, in his debut couture collection for Jean Paul Gaultier, used 3D printing to create a hyperrealistic distorted male torso with hand painted birthmarks and latex nipples.
Congratulations are in order for Pierpaolo Piccioli who showed his first couture collection for Balenciaga. By focusing on the dramatic silhouette of the house and eschewing stunts, he delivered that familiar airy refinement we came to expect from him at Valentino.
In all, this HCFW embraced innovation and history at the same time; Designers practiced using textures like silicon and plastic, they continued to utilize feathers in place of furs, and they pleated their little hearts out to create magnificent displays of craftsmanship. The future of haute couture seems bright.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Twelve years into her career, success has made Kriti Sanon (@kritisanon) more fearless, but not careless. “I have to instinctively feel that this is something I can’t let go of,” she says. The old fear of needing to constantly be on set has faded, replaced by a simple shift: “It is more about quality versus quantity now.”
For our Luxury Issue, that idea feels especially relevant. Luxury, at its most meaningful, isn’t only about access, but discernment: the ability to choose fewer, better things, and to pause without mistaking stillness for disappearance. Sanon doesn’t speak of this phase as though she’s arrived. “This is probably the most exciting phase of my life and my career so far,” she says, with gratitude in her voice — and relief, too.
Click the 🔗 in bio to read the complete interview.
On @kritisanon: Silk long-sleeve shirt, light wool single-breasted jacket, Blueprint silk twill culottes, Lauren 1980 clutch, all by @newbottega. Razzmatazz earrings, @forestofchintz. Ring by @litmusindia.
Editorial Director & Words by: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Anaita Shroff Adajania (@anaitashroffadajania)
Asst. Art Director: Shrushti Sudhakar (@shrushti_sudhakar) (Cover Design)
Makeup: Adrian Jacobs (@adrianjacobsofficial)
Hair: Aasif Ahmed (@aasifahmedofficial )
Nail: Bristle Dust (@bristle.dust)
Bookings Editor: Rishith Shetty (@rishithvshetty)

posts.alt_text:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStars: Pride is not a June-only conversation. It is shifting beyond visibility and asking what comes next. What does it mean to build communities that outlast individuals? To create institutions instead of exceptions? To leave behind spaces where the next person does not have to start from scratch?

For ELLE’s Pride portfolio, designer James Ferreira (@jamesferreiralabel), drag king and theatre-maker Siaan (@mxsiaan), athlete Dutee Chand (@duteechand) and transmedia producer Zena Sagar (@zena.sagar)  reflect on the futures they are trying to build. 

Their methods differ, but their ambition is remarkably similar: to leave behind a world more expansive than the one they inherited. Visibility may open the door, but it is community, institutions and shared power that keep it open.

Bringing these stories together is illustrator Vikalp Durga Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra), whose distinct visual language expands the portfolio into a shared queer world of colour, intimacy and imagination.
.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
.

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStars: Pride is not a June-only conversation. It is shifting beyond visibility and asking what comes next. What does it mean to build communities that outlast individuals? To create institutions instead of exceptions? To leave behind spaces where the next person does not have to start from scratch?
For ELLE’s Pride portfolio, designer James Ferreira (@jamesferreiralabel), drag king and theatre-maker Siaan (@mxsiaan), athlete Dutee Chand (@duteechand) and transmedia producer Zena Sagar (@zena.sagar)  reflect on the futures they are trying to build.
Their methods differ, but their ambition is remarkably similar: to leave behind a world more expansive than the one they inherited. Visibility may open the door, but it is community, institutions and shared power that keep it open.
Bringing these stories together is illustrator Vikalp Durga Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra), whose distinct visual language expands the portfolio into a shared queer world of colour, intimacy and imagination.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
Editorial Director: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Illustration: Vikalp Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra)
Words by : Tejaswi Subramanian (@tejnesss)
Cover Design: Shrushti Sudhakar (@shrushti_sudhakar)
Editorial Co-ordinator: Sneh Lad (@sneh_lad)
[Pride, Queer Rights]
posts.alt_text:
#ELLECoverStar: Twelve years into her career, success has made Kriti Sanon (@kritisanon) more fearless, but not careless. “I have to instinctively feel that this is something I can’t let go of,” she says. The old fear of needing to constantly be on set has faded, replaced by a simple shift: “It is more about quality versus quantity now.” 

For our Luxury Issue, that idea feels especially relevant. Luxury, at its most meaningful, isn’t only about access, but discernment: the ability to choose fewer, better things, and to pause without mistaking stillness for disappearance. Sanon doesn’t speak of this phase as though she’s arrived. “This is probably the most exciting phase of my life and my career so far,” she says, with gratitude in her voice — and relief, too.

Click the 🔗 in bio to read the complete interview.
.
On @kritisanon: Silk long-sleeve shirt, light wool single-breasted jacket, Blueprint silk twill culottes, Lauren 1980 clutch, all by @newbottega. Razzmatazz earrings, @forestofchintz. Ring by @litmusindia.

#ELLECoverStar: Victoria Harbour has been the face of Hong Kong for generations, where the city's soaring skyline meets the water in a view recognised around the world. It sets the stage for our latest cover shoot, with Jacqueline Fernandez (@jacquelienefernandez) bringing her unmistakable presence to one of the city's most iconic locations. As the skyline shifts with the light, she reminds us that the most captivating moments don't always ask for attention. Sometimes, they’re simply felt. Read our interview with the cover star at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ELLEShowTime: If there's one film everyone seems to be talking about this week, it's 'The Odyssey'. Timelines are flooded with clips of its visuals, debates over its adaptation of Homer's epic and, of course, the familiar Christopher Nolan discourse. Every new Nolan film arrives with the same questions: Will audiences understand it? Will it be another cinematic puzzle? But for years, another conversation has followed Nolan's films. From 'The Prestige' and 'The Dark Knight' to 'Inception' and even 'Oppenheimer', his female characters have often been criticised for existing more as catalysts for the men around them than as fully realised people in their own right. With 'The Odyssey' arriving in cinemas tomorrow, we're asking one question: Has Christopher Nolan finally learned to write women? Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the complete review.
Words by: @16ektasinha
Images via: @universalpicturesindia
[The Odyssey, Anne Hathaway, Zendaya, Charlize Theron, Lupita Nyong'o]

posts.caption:
#ELLECulture Few actors have had a style journey as memorable as their filmography, and Katrina Kaif is undoubtedly one of them. From cinematic looks that became part of pop culture to off-screen appearances that continue to inspire brides, minimalists and fashion lovers alike, she’s spent years proving that personal style doesn’t have to be loud to leave a lasting impression. Through the years, we’ve had the privilege of celebrating that journey across our pages and covers. Today, we’re raising a toast to the actor, entrepreneur and fashion icon whose elegance has always felt effortless. Happy Birthday Katrina Kaif !
[Katrina Kaif, Iconic outfits]

posts.caption:
#ELLECulture: The social media manager is live-posting from an event at midnight. The PR executive is handling a crisis before breakfast. The marketing manager has somehow attended three meetings, written a deck and replied to 47 emails before lunch and it’s still not enough. Everyone is exhausted. Everyone is overbooked. And everyone is secretly a little proud of it. In most industries, burnout is considered a problem, don’t get us wrong, it is one, but in ours, it’s often treated like proof of relevance. More about being a SPAM girl at the 🔗 in bio.
Words by: @vishakha_punjabi
[SPAM girls, Working, Creative Jobs, 2000s]
#Burnout #SPAM #Media #2000s

posts.caption:
Welcome to the next era of @karolg.
Following her historic Coachella performance in April—where she became the first Latina to headline the annual music festival—the Grammy-winning Colombian pop star has been rehearsing endlessly for her biggest international stadium tour yet: Viajando por el Mundo Tropitour. Following her previous Mañana Será Bonito tour—the fifth-highest-grossing tour by a Latin artist of all time—her new show will be both a celebration of the Latin community and an expansion of her sound and performance. “People try to pigeonhole me, but I’m so many things,” she tells ELLE. “I love that my image and what I represent are deeply Latin, yet I also want to be a global artist.”
In ELLE’s Summer 2026 digital cover story, the global superstar opens up about her pride in representing the Latin community, finding herself through love and loss, and teases her next musical era: “The next version of me will be new to people, but it’s always been there.”
Read the full interview at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Elliot and Erick Jiménez @elliotanderick
Stylist: Nycole Sariol @nycolesariol
Writer: Suzy Exposito @brujacore
Hair: Dindi Hojah at Forward Artists @dindihojah
Makeup: Bo for Dior Beauty @bo_champagne
Production: Nathalie Akiya and Raz Segal at GTS Productions @gtsprod
posts.alt_text:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.

Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”

For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw
Writer: Kayla Webley Adler @kaylaw
Hair: Orlando Pita at Orlo Salon @hairbyorlandopita @orlosalon

posts.caption: Fashion wasn’t the only thing sparkling during Paris Couture Week. Between the runway shows, the city became a treasure box of extraordinary jewelry unveilings from leading maisons, where inventive settings, vibrant color combinations, and exceptional savoir-faire took center stage. From @mikimoto’s fire opal brooch mixed with pearls and diamonds to the elevated “Signes and Symboles” collection from @chanel high jewelry, the standout creations prioritized sculptural silhouettes and extraordinary stone pairings. Here, ELLE executive fashion and accessories director @awolfestyle spotlights the week’s unforgettable jewelry moments.

posts.caption:
It’s ‘The Odyssey’ week—and ahead of Christopher Nolan epic’s theatrical debut this Friday, we welcomed friends of the magazine to an intimate private screening at AMC Lincoln Square in New York City. The evening celebrated one of summer’s most anticipated releases while bringing together designers, creatives, and industry insiders for an early look at the film.
The event marked the latest collaboration between ELLE and Universal following our Summer 2026 issue spotlighting the film’s leading women: @annehathaway, @zendaya, @lupitanyongo, and @charlizeafrica. Swipe through for an inside look and tap the link in bio for more.
Photos: Shelby Pine @shelbypine

posts.caption:
Talk about a power duo: On Wednesday, ELLE editor-in-chief @ninagarcia sat down with our Summer 2026 digital cover star @karolg as part of the #FIFAWorldCup Playmakers Summit. It was a beautiful conversation that flowed between English and Spanish and demonstrated a lovely affection between the two. (When I had the opportunity to celebrate this announcement of your fourth tour, I was like, ‘Yes, let’s do it.’ I’m so excited. I will be there every step, baby!” Garcia said.)
During the course of their discussion—facilitated by @telemundo and @nbcuniversal—Karol reflected on being the first Latina to headline Coachella, her upcoming stadium tour, and the way that music can bring people together. At the link in bio, read our main takeaways from the conversation.

posts.caption:
After finishing her 2025 novel ‘Atmosphere,’ @tjenkinsreid really thought she was done for a while. Then, a fateful shower changed everything.
“I just went, ‘Pool hustlers,’” she tells ELLE. “For me, the question of whether to write a story or not is always this question of, ‘Do I see it?’ And I just saw it.” That vague image of pool hustlers eventually grew into the premise for her new novel, ‘The Last Days of Vic & Coco,’ out March 2, 2027, as ELLE can exclusively reveal.
Reid had always wanted to write about female friendship, and “the specific way that women platonically love one another,” she says, “and the devastation that only friendship can bring to somebody’s heart.” Now, with ‘Vic & Coco,’ was her chance.
In a wide-ranging conversation, Reid goes deep about the relationships in her life that inspired the book’s characters, her research process, and the narrative thread all her novels share. Tap the link in bio to read the full interview.

posts.caption:
Your date might be smiling at you across the table, but she has already clocked the shoes, the body language, the outfit and whether that hug felt natural or like a corporate handshake. The first ten minutes of a date are less about compatibility and more about one simple question: do I want to spend the next two hours with this person or fake an early morning tomorrow? The ick is rarely one dramatic moment. More often it is something you did unknowingly that is insignificant to you can but can suddenly become the reason there isn’t a second date.
Here is what not to do in those crucial minutes.
#EsquireIndia #FirstDate

posts.caption:
On a sweltering afternoon in Bandra, inside an old heritage bungalow with a heat wave warning in effect, nearly 2,000 vinyl records from around the world were laid out for browsing at The Vinyl Pop Up (@thevinylpopup ), and the room was full. If buying physical music in the age of Spotify seems like an anachronistic pursuit, what’s even more surprising was the number of under-30s flipping through the crates.
We spoke to the collectors, crate diggers and first-time buyers to understand what keeps them coming back to vinyl in the streaming era. Read the full story at the link in bio.
Written by: Kashish Mishra (@kashish.mishra_)
#EsquireIndia #VinylRecords

posts.caption:
#TheOdysseyMovieReview: The Iliad and The Odyssey are enormous stories - there is war, politics, lust, magic, prophecy, grief, nearly every element of human experience ever expressed since the dawn of time. Christopher Nolan’s film, on an IMAX screen, is visually as massive as anything there is. And yet it never feels massive. It takes a while to understand why that isn’t a flaw: the scale never overwhelms the human heart at its core.
But more than anything, the film feels like proof that ancient stories still work, and that our attention spans haven’t gone anywhere.
Read the full review at the link in bio.
Written by: Mayukh Majumdar (@mayuxkh)
#EsquireIndia #TheOdyssey #TheOdysseyReview

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.caption: It absolutely is a trademark infringement, made more certain by the 7/11 date the sneaker is to drop in stores. However, I would handle the situation differently. I would ask for the same restitution in my complaint demand but I would have included a collaborative clause /ADR in my lawsuit demand letter, offering a "pre-suit mediation" provision allowing for us to enter into a brand collaboration/ licensing agreement once the other terms have been satisfied. This way as a plaintiff you set yourself up from the outset as “resolution-focused” over protracted litigation. This gives you a leg up over the defense as being a more responsible party and it will resonate with a judge / jury.

posts.caption:
For nearly two decades, our cover star, Yami Gautam Dhar, has built a career on one simple principle: never be predictable. From romantic dramas to thrillers, from characters who quietly endure to women who command the narrative, she has consistently chosen reinvention over repetition. Thoughtful, fearless, and deeply committed to her craft, Yami continues to challenge both herself and her audience with every role she takes on. Just a few reasons why we stan!

posts.caption:
“You know, there comes a time at a very crucial point in your life when you become absolutely fearless and just want to work without having anything else on your mind. The only thing I think about is whether I will love doing what I’ve just committed to. Of course, you want the outcome to be proportionate to your passion, but I also know it is not in my hands. What is in my control is to make sure that whatever I say ‘yes’ to, I’m happy to do, to enjoy it thoroughly.”

posts.caption:
"The older I get, the more I love doing the small, simple things. I love going for a walk with my husband Rohan. If it were up to me, then, through that day, I would literally only want to sit in bed and read a book, like one of those smutty rom-com books from start to finish. That is essentially what a day off for me looks like, where I’m recalibrating and charging my own batteries with some alone time because I genuinely love my own company."

Anshula Kapoor has never claimed to have all the answers, and perhaps that's exactly what makes her so relatable. Fresh off her wedding, the entrepreneur, creator, and mental health advocate continues to build a community by showing up exactly as she is, embracing vulnerability, celebrating growth, and reminding us that healing is rarely linear. Beyond the public perception and the famous surname is a woman who continues to choose hope, even on the hardest days, inspiring others to do the same.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous. 

Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”

Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
In reviewing newly rated brands over the past three months, we’ve got some good news and bad news 😬⁠
⁠
The good news? Of the 431 brands rated, 82 (19%) of rated brands received our two highest scores of “Good” and “Great”. This is an improvement from Q1, where just 8% of the 362 brands scored our highest 💫⁠
⁠
The bad news? 122 brands rated (28%) received our lowest rating of “We Avoid”. This number is technically more than the lowest rated brands in Q1, where 113 (31%) had our lowest rating.⁠
⁠
Who are these top performing, more sustainable brands? Swipe to meet them, from bridalwear from the UK to a slow fashion goth company from the US 💚⁠
⁠
Share any brands you want to see rated, in case we’re able to get through them this quarter. Visit the link for the full story too →↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #SustainableFashion #Sustainability #OOTD #SummerStyle

posts.caption:
Wouldn’t it be nice if you could drop old clothes into your garden and grow a tree? 🌳⁠
⁠
Unfortunately for now, that idea is a fantasy as compostable clothing remains in a distant future due to myriad hurdles. But the demand is there: more and more people are turning to composting as an example of a regenerative future in the present, where composting offers a clever means to keep fashion circular ⏺️⁠
⁠
Compostable fashion may not be a reality now, but many communities are pushing for this to become a reality—and brands are taking notice, to mixed results. From Stella McCartney to Under Armor to @wearkent, initiatives are in motion—but some are more performative than others.⁠
⁠
If you’re interested in a regenerative fashion future, watch the composting space. Start by reading a new story by Fru Bekefi at the link, who explores how this technique could emerge in the coming years 🌱↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #Sustainability #SustainableFashion #Composting #Nature
