Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

posts.caption:
Converse are a classic—but it’s not very ethical 😔⁠
⁠
Our recent review of the brand across its impact on planet, people, and animals has yielded some disappointing results as it’s making minimal efforts across two key categories. Swipe to learn about them ⏩⁠
⁠
For such a big brand, Converse has more than enough resources to implement change while becoming a leader in championing industry change. If you’re curious about similar brands that *are* making change, visit the link to explore our more ethical shoe guide 👟↑⁠
⁠
#Sneakers #Converse #Shoes #Sustainability #SustainableFashion
posts.alt_text:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

The year is 2006. Celebrity Big Brother is on every night, there’s Baden-Baden World Cup fever, and a halter neck top with boot-cut jeans is your uniform for drinks at the local pub. Simpler times.
It’s also been 20 years this week since @lilyallen released her debut album All Right, Still. From classics like Smile, LDN, Knock ‘Em Out and Alfie, it gave us some of the decade’s most memorable songs. Which track is your forever favourite? Comment below 👇

posts.caption:
Well, that was Messi 👀
England have been knocked out of the men’s World Cup by Argentina, who will now face Spain on Sunday in the final. It’s not coming home - but there’s still so much to celebrate. A record-breaking tournament with Harry Kane still England’s all-time leading scorer, and Jude Bellingham the first player since Diego Maradona in 1986 to score two goals in consecutive World Cup knockout matches. You’ll get ‘em next time, lads.

posts.caption: Whatever England's fate this week, the tournament has offered hope to fans who like their sport a little less macho – whether it's the affectionate friendship of Jude Bellingham and Erling Haaland or the latter's choice to recognise his athlete mother on his shirt. Toxic masculinity is a serious problem in football, with three players in the World Cup currently facing sexual assault allegations, and no openly gay male players in England’s top four men’s divisions. That's why we must celebrate the players who support each other and lift up women – and who might just restore our faith in the beautiful game, says @charleyross. Read more at the link in bio.

And it is not just on the red carpet. From micro pyjama shorts at The Odyssey photocall against the Eiffel Tower to slouchy pink tailored trousers paired with an even pinker, fuzzy clutch, Theron has been delivering one memorable look after another. Safe to say, she has earned a permanent place on our outfit moodboards.

posts.caption:
Fashion has no shortage of beautiful faces, but @alexconsani represents a new generation of supermodels—the ones not just walking the runway but creating platforms, building communities, and leading with personality. First gaining attention on TikTok, Consani is now one of the world’s most sought after models. “I’m having fun,” says Consani. “I’m doing it for all 50 of the other bitches that would love to be in that room.” For Bazaar’s Summer 2026 issue, the supermodel talks about trying her hand at acting, moving into her big-girl home, and entering her “experimental era.”

posts.caption: As @sean.mcgirr prepares to bring @alexandermcqueen back to #LondonFashionWeek, the creative director is drawing on the city’s music, art and notoriously messy nightlife to shape the house’s next chapter. In many ways this is a return to form for the brand. Come fashion week in September, the brand will be returning not simply to a geographic location but to the place where its visual language was formed: a city of collision, theatricality and disorder. McGirr’s task now is not to recreate the London Lee McQueen once knew, but to listen closely enough to the city that exists now. At the link in our bio, @osman_ahmed_ received all the details on how he is tackling this task and the places where he is sourcing his inspiration.

posts.caption:
Cargo shorts are having a real moment this summer, and @whitemountaineering_official Spring/Summer 2027 collection makes a strong case for why. The brand's first collection since founder Yosuke Aizawa's departure features technical takes on the classic silhouette, from corded khaki styles with slanted pockets to denim pairs finished with oversized zip cargo compartments.⁠
⁠
Far beyond their military roots, cargo shorts now exist at every level, from dependable staples by Dickies and Carhartt to luxury versions from CELINE, Chrome Hearts, and Dior. Whether they're packed with gear or worn completely pocket-empty, one thing is clear: cargo shorts are once again the shorts to beat this summer.

posts.caption:
When @rorymcilroy arrived at the 2019 Open Championship wearing a @nike x @stoneisland jacket, it was something of an outlier.⁠
⁠
Golf was still years away from the explosion of fashion brands, collaborations and broader cultural interest that would follow. Luxury labels weren't part of the conversation, and seeing Stone Island in a Wednesday practice round was genuinely unexpected. Looking back now, the outfit feels like more of a preview of what was to come.⁠
2019年の全英オープンに、@rorymcilroy が  @nike x @stoneisland のジャケットを着用して姿を現したとき、それは当時としては異例の出来事だった。
まだゴルフは、ファッションブランドとのコラボレーションや、カルチャーとの結びつきが急速に広がる以前の時代。ラグジュアリーブランドがゴルフシーンで語られることも少なく、水曜日の練習ラウンドでStone Islandを目にすること自体が新鮮な驚きだった。
今振り返ると、あのスタイルは、これから訪れるゴルフとファッションの新しい時代を予告していたようにも思える。
Photo: David Davies/Richard Sellers/Getty Images
posts.alt_text:
@lewishamilton and @minwoo27lee both occupy very different corners of the sporting world, but the two @lululemon ambassadors share more than a competitive drive. ⁠
⁠
Both have built careers that extend beyond their respective sports. Hamilton through fashion, music and film; Lee through a growing presence at the intersection of golf, style and social media. Just as importantly, they're both defined by a curiosity that pushes them beyond their comfort zones.⁠
⁠
That idea sits at the center of "Rest Day" with Lewis Hamilton, lululemon's new content series pairing the seven-time Formula 1 World Champion with elite athletes from different disciplines.⁠
⁠
Ahead of the series premiere, Hypegolf caught up with Lee to discuss what surprised him most about teaching Hamilton, why golf continues to resonate with elite athletes and the advice that immediately started changing the seven-time world champion's swing.⁠

Read the full interview at the link in bio.
⁠
Photo/video: lululemon

posts.caption:
@olaoluslawn has transformed 10 @ravennafootballclub jerseys and two pairs of Nike R10s into one-of-one artworks for the new SLAWN Limited Edition Capsule.⁠
⁠
Designed by @giorgiomallone and reworked in Slawn’s London studio, each jersey is unique and co-signed by the artist and @ronaldinho. The project is the latest chapter of Ronaldinho x Ravenna by @Cipriani, bringing football, art and contemporary culture together.⁠
⁠
The collection will be auctioned through MatchWornShirt, with a portion of proceeds supporting @streetsoccerusa.⁠
⁠
Credit: @Cipriani
posts.alt_text:
“Men do think they’re beautiful,” @mickalenethomas tells @hypebeast in her Brooklyn studio. “It’s okay to show your sensitive side. But that’s not always afforded to some Black men within their own communities.”

The Jersey-born, New York-based artist has spent the last two decades rewriting the rules of Black portraiture. Her bold, rhinestone-encrusted multimedia portraits of women in opulent interiors have lifted her to the top of the contemporary art game. And now, her gaze is turning elsewhere.

In comes ‘Beneath the Moon,’ her first exhibition devoted to Black masculinity. Now up at @theshepherddetroit, here, Thomas reworks an editorial commission into her signature monumental, rewiring canonical motifs into new visions of glamor, agency, and possibility. 

For our latest Hypeart Visits, we caught up with the artist to learn more about her muses, the origin of her rhinestones, and what it means to turn softness into strength. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

Photo: Studio portraits by Lila Barth. All images courtesy of the artist and @librarystreetcollective.

posts.caption:
You may have noticed an influx in custom World Cup outfits on your timeline this past month, and there’s a reason. Since @tolami_benson lit up Euro 2024 in her bespoke looks from @antonia.bronze and @hattiecrowther, WAG fashion has been defined by the art of upcycling.
Some of the best looks of the tournament so far have come from our favorite WAGs, working with different designers to transform jerseys into one-of-a-kind statement pieces for each game.  From Norway to Brazil, WAGs are officially fashion icons again, and they’re changing the way fans dress up, too.
Hypebae’s Associate Editor @s.imi gives us a brief timeline of WAG style and where the new obsession with upcycling fits in. Swipe to see some of the best looks of the tournament.
Imagery: Courtesy of Brands

posts.caption:
If you’ve come across a scrunched T-shirt or a reversible bomber jacket on your Instagram feed, you’re likely familiar with Scarlet & Sam. Born from one custom crochet top and an overwhelming response online, the Aussie wardrobe staple has garnered an impressive community of fashion fans across the globe.
Known for signature styles like the Capsule Bomber and the Juicy Wedge, Scarlet & Sam is a brand that takes wardrobe basics to the next level through thoughtfully considered details and subtle tweaks.
As the brand gears up for its Aussie winter, we caught up with founder Scarlet Robertson to find out more about the story behind Scarlet & Sam, its best-selling styles and its strategy for going global.  Images: Scarlet & Sam

posts.caption:
Summer doesn’t arrive all at once. It unfolds in moods, moments, and memories. ☀️🌿
@ahsaassy_ steps into the season’s softest chapter, where easy silhouettes and unhurried afternoons become a reminder that slowing down is its own kind of luxury.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this explores the evolving moods of summer, from nature’s quiet embrace to the intensity of the afternoon sun, and the effortless cool of golden evenings.✨
This is a celebration of the season in all its shades, beginning with the calm before the heat, where style isn’t about standing out, but feeling completely at home in your own skin.

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
It's simply too hot to do a full beat anyways.
On July 14th, you couldn't scroll on the internet without seeing #Zendaya. Not just because of her jaw-dropping @matieresfecales look, but also for the minimal, angelic glam that accompanied it. But Z isn't the only one adopting a fresher face for the red carpet. Stars like #SabrinaCarpenter, #AlexaDemie, and #Gabbriette have been hitting the town in "no-makeup" glam for months. Hell, #PamelaAnderson has ditched her makeup kit completely over the last couple of years. While people online swore that "clean-girl"  makeup was done and 2016-inspired full glam was back earlier in the year, it seems many it-girls beg to differ.
📷: @gettyimages

posts.alt_text:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum). 

Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood. 

Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.

At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
With an exciting career ahead of her, when Sahher Bambba (@sahherbambba) sets her gaze on the future, she envisions some big changes, save for the hope she wishes to retain across her journey.
“I hope that the scale of my work changes and the roles that are coming my way are even more exciting and even more challenging. I want to grow as an actor because at no point do I wish to get complacent. So the endeavour would be to just keep growing and growing, and as an actor. So I just hope that work-wise the challenges get bigger. But what I hope does not change is the curiosity and the excitement I feel every time that I step onto a set,” says our cover star.
Read the full cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
Is there anything that still scares Sahher Bambba (@sahherbambba) about the industry? “The thing that one needs to understand is the uncertainty of the profession. One day you could have a lot of work, and the next day you’ll be out of work. This profession is a lot about phases. And you very well know it’s going to happen before even entering the industry. I wouldn’t say it scares me but sometimes it gives me jitters,” explains our cover star.
Read the full cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
In an industry defined by constant highs and lows, resilience is a skill that comes with time. Sahher’s (@sahherbambba) trials have turned her into a believer. “Initially, when you’re starting out, because you’ve seen so many rejections and so many lows, more than highs, you reach a point where things don’t bother you. Earlier, if there was a part that I was about to get and maybe I didn’t get, one or two weeks go for a toss.”
She adds, “Now, I’ve reached a point where I’ve started to believe in destiny. What is meant to be yours will be yours. A lot of people, all of Mumbai, had auditioned to play Karishma Talwar. I would like to believe destiny played its role. I got to be a part of the project. You can’t force what isn’t yours. This understanding and realisation gives me a lot of peace.”
Editor: Analita Seth (@analitaseth)

Un rovescio a una mano, raro nel tennis di oggi, e un gioco elegante e variato: @Lore_Musetti, 24 anni, ha aperto il 2026 conquistando il quinto posto del ranking ATP, prima che un infortunio alla gamba sinistra lo costringesse a rinunciare al Roland Garros. Global ambassador di #BottegaVeneta, il tennista si racconta a @LOfficielItalia, ritratto dagli scatti di @RaffGrosso e con lo styling di @Fernando.Echeverria_, vestendo un total look della collezione #Fall2026, firmato dalla direttrice creativa @Louise_Trotter_. Tra scaramanzie da spogliatoio, il bronzo olimpico di Parigi, l'ammirazione per il tennis di Alcaraz e la passione per la cucina: «Colpire la pallina, il suono che fa quando la colpisco: è questo che mi rende felice». Leggi l’intervista a cura di @Fabia.DiDrusco al link in bio e sul nuovo numero de @LOfficielItalia, ora in edicola.

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!
The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.
From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.
Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”
Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

posts.caption:
Una voce italiana al centro del mondo. Domenica 19 luglio, al MetLife Stadium di New York, Laura Pausini sale sul palco della cerimonia di chiusura dei #FIFAWorldCup2026 al fianco di Robbie Williams e Nicole Scherzinger. Insieme cantano «Desire», inno ufficiale della FIFA: quel brano che intreccia inglese e spagnolo, con l’adattamento firmato dalla stessa Pausini.
Non un debutto, un ritorno. Sullo stesso palco dove, un anno fa, aveva eseguito il brano con Williams per la prima volta.
«Sono orgogliosa che una voce italiana faccia parte di questa celebrazione globale», ha scritto la cantante. E in fondo è tutto qui: musica, calcio, #Desire. La stessa emozione che unisce le persone.
Photo: L’OFFICIEL / NICHOLAS FOLS & SIERMOND
#LauraPausini #RobbieWilliams #NicoleScherzinger

posts.caption:
«Io sono Patty Pravo e lo sarò per sempre». L’eterna ragazza del Piper si racconta a L’OFFICIEL ITALIA in un’intervista a cura di @cadeaudavril.
Venezia all’origine, Parigi nel cuore, il palco come unico posto dove il tempo smette di contare. «Nascere nell’arte e nella bellezza sicuramente ti forma in un certo modo», racconta. Con “Opera”, il suo ultimo lavoro discografico, torna a fare quello che ha sempre fatto meglio, sorprendere senza volerlo, emozionare senza cercarlo. In mezzo, l’addio cantato a Ornella Vanoni sul palco dell’Ariston, il ricordo di Gianni Versace, e il front row al Grand Palais per il debutto di Matthieu Blazy da Chanel. «Io sono sempre stata me stessa, non ho mai pensato a stupire o sorprendere per il gusto di farlo. Mi sono semplicemente espressa». Ecco Patty Pravo, in tutta la sua indomabile libertà.
Il nostro nuovo numero vi aspetta in edicola!

posts.caption:
«”Opera” nasce Da un sogno. Poi, il mio assistente Simone ha visualizzato l’intera estetica e ha chiamato Giovanni Caccamo alle quattro del mattino. Così è nata. Quando l’ho ascoltata mi è piaciuta subito, e così è diventata il primo brano dell’album. È un progetto musicale di grande ricerca, con testi che sfiorano l’anima e sonorità che non finiscono mai di sorprendere. Ha anche una copertina che reputo bellissima. Opera nell’opera, un ritorno al sublime».
Leggi l’intervista completa di Patty Pravo a cura di @cadeaudavril su L’OFFICIEL ITALIA.

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
The world’s biggest luxury brands generate billions in sales every year, but only a handful dominate the global market.
From Louis Vuitton, Chanel, and Hermès to Rolex, Dior, and Cartier, these iconic maisons continue to lead the industry through exceptional craftsmanship, timeless design, and unparalleled global demand. Together, they set the benchmark for modern luxury while shaping trends across fashion, jewellery, watches, and lifestyle.
Here are the biggest luxury brands in the world ranked by annual sales.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Chelsea Residences by DAMAC has officially launched its sixth and final tower, marking the last opportunity to own a home in the world's first football-branded residential development. The first five towers have already sold out.
Located in Dubai Maritime City, the waterfront project brings Chelsea Football Club's legacy into luxury living through Chelsea-inspired design, panoramic sea views and exclusive amenities including a rooftop football pitch, athlete training centre and wellness spaces.
With prices starting from AED 2.56 million, the development is redefining branded luxury real estate and reinforcing Dubai's position as a global destination for iconic residential projects.
@damacofficial  @ali_saj
#DAMAC

posts.caption:
Taj has once again been crowned India’s strongest brand, earning the title for the fifth consecutive year.
The iconic luxury hospitality brand secured the top position in the Brand Finance India 100 2026 rankings, reflecting its continued leadership in customer trust, brand value, and global reputation. From landmark heritage palaces to award-winning luxury resorts and international expansions, Taj has spent more than a century defining Indian hospitality at its finest.
The recognition reinforces Taj’s position not only as India’s most respected hotel brand, but as one of the country’s most valuable global luxury icons.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Lionel Messi’s success extends far beyond the football pitch.
While millions know him as one of the greatest players in history, few realize he’s also built a growing luxury hospitality portfolio across Europe. Through MiM Hotels, Messi owns a collection of boutique properties in destinations including Ibiza, Mallorca, Sitges, Baqueira, Sotogrande, and Andorra. The hotels are known for their contemporary design, premium wellness experiences, and prime locations, and are now managed by Meliá Hotels International under The Melia Collection, further elevating their luxury positioning.
From beachfront escapes to alpine retreats, Messi’s hotel empire reflects a long-term vision beyond football-proving that some of his biggest investments are made far away from the stadium.
@melia.hotels.international #Luxurious

posts.caption:
His work has brought him to countless destinations across the globe, but @TravisScott still has one major stop left on his bucket list. “I hit every continent on the last tour except Antarctica,” he says. “It’s kind of crazy.”
For Scott, travel is about more than seeing new places—it’s about expanding perspective, and he encourages leaning into exploration whenever possible. “Go touch earth and go actually interact with this world,” he says. “It could change your mindset.”
Read more about Scott’s adventures at the link in our bio.

posts.alt_text:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!

The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.

From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.

Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”

Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

Global Chief Content Officer @giampietrobaudo

posts.caption:
The 2026 July/August issue is here!
At the center of this year’s Summer Entertainment Issue is @TravisScott, who opens up about working on the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia,’ the travels and experiences fueling his next chapter, and making his acting debut as part of a star-studded ensemble in Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey.’
As much as we love a blockbuster with a stacked A-list cast, championing fresh talent is always top of mind. Inside the issue, we spotlight eight rising stars whose work this summer demands your attention, from ‘Outer Banks’ star @MadisonBaileyBabe to Avantika of ‘Not Suitable for Work’ to @sashacalle of ‘Sugar.’
It’s also a blockbuster time for literary escapes, as the rise of #BookTok turns reading into an unexpected status symbol that’s shaping summer travel. Looking ahead to the Fall collections, we explore the age-old question of whether sex still sells, given the state of undress on the runway, red carpet, and social feeds in 2026. Stay tuned for the issue hitting newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
On the 29th anniversary of Gianni Versace’s passing, we’re revisiting eight design signatures that made him unmistakably him — the eight commandments of @versace, if you will.
At a time when many of his peers favored austere minimalism, #GianniVersace stayed true to himself. His flashy signature looks set him apart from anyone else in the fashion world: bondage-inspired gowns, vibrant prints, and metallic miniskirts. Ever the innovator, when a technique or fabric he wanted didn’t exist, he simply invented it.
From the Medusa logo to the famed ‘90s bondage collection, Gianni’s mark on the house lives on through public memory and the cyclical nature of fashion trends.
Read more on his design signatures at the link in our bio.
Photos Getty Images
#versace #gianniversacearchive #versacearchives

posts.caption:
⁠A new era for @Marni begins with a love letter to its own archive.⁠
⁠
@meryllrogge.personal has officially taken the helm as creative director — and her Fall/Winter 2026 debut at Milan Fashion Week makes her mission clear: not to reproduce Marni's past, but to revive its spirit. The Belgian-born designer, an Antwerp Royal Academy alum and seven-year veteran of the Marc Jacobs studio, spent months in the house's archives before landing on silhouettes defined by natural shoulders and softly dropped waistlines — a nod to the beloved Marni of the late '90s and early 2000s. "I've been a devotee of Marni since my teens," says Rogge. "It is the very landscape of my fashion identity."⁠
⁠
But it was a move to Milan, not the archives, that truly shaped her debut. "I wasn't interested in reproducing Marni's past. I wanted to revive its spirit, its emotional core," she explains. "In Belgium and Northern Europe, people are more relaxed. In Milan, people truly make an effort and dress extremely well every day." Read more from Rogge on her journey, her runway debut, and what's next for Marni — link in bio.⁠
⁠
Writer Simone Vertua⁠
⁠
Photos courtesy of Maciej Kucia and Marni⁠
⁠
#Marni #MeryllRogge

posts.caption:
Why is hypersexual fashion dominating runways, red carpets, and pop culture? Welcome to fashion’s biggest contradiction.
Naked dressing—skimpy, sheer, strategically undone—has quietly gone from scandal to formula. From Gigi Hadid’s sheer @miumiu at the Met to #Demna’s diamond-thonged @Gucci debut, this season’s biggest looks share one thread: skin as statement, not shock value.
Fashion historian @valerie_steele_fashion points to a decade-long shift, noting that hypersexual fashion “has become more and more normalized again in the last maybe 10 years” — with major designers leading the charge. The proof is everywhere: @matthieu_blazy’s subversive see-through mesh @chanelofficial suit, and the bodycon, day-after dishevelment, and diamond thongs that reigned at Gucci’s Fall/Winter 2026 debut.
But here’s the twist — as the red carpet bares more, culture is doing the opposite. Call it a sex recession, or perhaps a shift toward conservatism—but the runway has always existed to reflect or subvert what’s going on off the runway. Read more at the link in our bio.
Writer @jessicakroy
Photos courtesy of Getty Images and HBO

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.
For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”
Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

#ManifestCover

Marriage didn’t change Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero). It simply gave their love a new address.

“We dated for seven years and knew everything about each other before marriage,” says Zaheer.

“That’s why we got married,” Sonakshi adds. “We wanted to learn to co-exist, to live in harmony and happiness.”
Perhaps that’s what makes their story feel so effortless. Not the absence of disagreements, but the certainty that they’ll always find their way back to each other.

Our July-August issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.

For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”

Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.

Sometimes, all you need is a second to fall in love, and a lifetime to stay that way. And if a destination played Cupid here, Sri Lanka—with its sun-soaked shores, pristine beaches and idyllic views of the coast—would be the ultimate matchmaker.
According to WedMeGood’s Destination Wedding Report 2026, Sri Lanka has emerged as one of the fastest-growing overseas wedding spots, owing to several factors—proximity, luxury hospitality and experience-led celebrations.
We take a look at the lure that this island nation has on the wedding scene.

posts.caption:
The soundtrack of the Big Fat Indian Wedding is getting a global upgrade.
Luxury Indian weddings are moving beyond Bollywood medleys, with international DJs, Boiler Room-style stages, immersive live performances and festival-inspired entertainment becoming the new gold standard. From Francis Mercier’s (@francismercier) Afro-house set at a Mumbai wedding to global names like Shimza (@shimza.dj), Hugel (@hugelthug) and Kahani (@kahanimusic), couples are taking inspiration from festivals like Coachella (@coachella), Sunburn (@sunburnfestival) and Ibiza (@ibizaibiza) wedding into an experience.
And it’s not just about who’s behind the console anymore. Traditional DJ booths are being replaced by festival-style main stages, immersive lighting, premium sound systems, live drummers, saxophonists and MCs. Every detail—from talent scouting to technical production—is designed to deliver a world-class show.
Because in 2026, the dance floor isn’t just part of the wedding. It’s the main event.
Read more via the link in bio🔗
By Mirza Shaina Beg (@mirzashainaa)
[Techno, Technomusic, Dance, Rave]

posts.caption:
When it comes to wedding style, nothing can dethrone the good old potli bag. But this wedding season, there’s a new contender manifesting itself in mainstream wedding fashion—the classic batua bag. Vintage, timeless and celebrity-approved, this old-fashioned coin purse is having a major revival, proving that heritage accessories never really go out of style.
From Khushi Kapoor carrying it twice during Anshula Kapoor’s pre-wedding festivities to Alia Bhatt, Sonam Kapoor and Athiya Shetty—Bollywood’s best-dressed stars are embracing the batua in every way possible.
If your wedding wardrobe is due for an accessory upgrade, it might just be time to bid adieu to potlis and say hello to batua bags. Bonus? You can carry it like a clutch or opt for one with a dainty chain string, making it as versatile as it is elegant.
Bags: Meera Mahadevia (@meera_mahadevia)
By Palak Valecha (@_palakvalecha_)
[bridal Accessories, Ethnic Handbags, Indian Wedding Fashion, Designer Handbags, Festive Fashion, Traditional Indian Craftsmanshipindian Couture]

posts.caption:
Wedding menus are became love stories on a plate and we are ready for another serve! Food has evolved from being just a lavish buffet to becoming one of the biggest storytelling elements of luxury Indian weddings. Instead of focusing on the number of dishes, couples are creating deeply personal, curated dining experiences that reflect their identities, memories, travels, heritage, and values.
Regional Indian cuisine is making a comeback, from hyper-local and grandmother’s recipes to traditional cooking techniques and forgotten delicacies. And young couples are proud to showcase their hometown cuisines alongside international dishes.
Read the full story via link in bio
By Ayandrali Dutta (@aaa.blah__nari)
[Big Fat Indian Wedding, Wedding Trends and Inspiration, Food storytelling, Culinary experience, Gourmet, Authentic, Experentail]

posts.caption:
When most women think of Chanel ballet flats, they think of them in their prim and proper Rue Cambon element. The classic tan-and-black pair have a reputation as polished and Parisian or manicured and uptown.
But recently, a subset of creative, upwardly mobile women are traipsing around in the flats in flashy colors and funky materials. Often in physical jobs and on set for production or styling, these women wear the shoes with baggy cargos, blooming skirts or low-slung jeans, rather than the tweed skirt suits they may have been previously imagined with.
“They’re like a utility shoe,” Ariella Starkman, a creative strategist and producer in New York, said. “They add a level of polish when I’m doing the dirty work,” she added.
Unlike luxury bags, shoes often do not appreciate in value. But Chanel flats appear to be a different story. Tap the link in our bio to read more about how Chanel flats have become the shoe du jour for fashion girls. Photos by Getty Images
posts.alt_text:
After a couple years of embracing tiny inseams, micro shorts and hot pants, shorts have grown long again. The trend-cycle pendulum has swung all the way back to an 1800s invention: the culotte. 

The culotte is, historically and by definition, a trouser masquerading as a skirt. The style gained mainstream appeal in the late 1960s and throughout the ’70s, finding fans in Diana Ross, the model Twiggy and the fashion designer Marc Bohan, who designed a pair made from fur when he was at Christian Dior. 

Recently, retailers and brands have introduced silhouettes they describe as culottes, like the long Bermuda shorts from the Gap; Bottega Veneta’s divided A-line skirts; and a trouser whose hem is slowly inching up the leg, ending mid-calf, like the ones sold by Veronica Beard. 

Today’s culottes are appealing across demographics and are helping solve a number of fashion crises. Tap the link in our bio to read the history of the culotte and how the style made its return. Photos by Star Max/GC Images, via Getty Images, @simbarashecha, Dave Benett/Getty Images, Estrop/Getty Images

posts.caption:
A reader wrote to our fashion critic: "I have my mother’s satin wedding gown, worn on Oct. 25, 1941 — just six weeks before Pearl Harbor. It’s magnificent, with long sleeves, ruching, satin buttons and a long skirt. I wore it in 1979 and had it preserved, hopefully for a future generation. That has not come to pass, and I struggle with what to do. I can’t see giving it to Salvation Army. Apparently it cannot be dyed. I’ve talked to a tailor about using the skirt material to make one or two blouses. It feels as if it should go into some kind of costume museum, but it’s not a big name designer. Am I being too sentimental?"
Clothes are powerful wormholes to our memories and emotions, which is why it can be so hard to throw them away. But is there liberation in letting go of the past? At the link in our bio, read @vvfriedman's advice. Photo via Jainel Morris

posts.caption:
The summer edition of haute couture isn’t for the faint of heart. It’s always hot outside, and heading indoors seldom provides much relief. Spare a thought for the models, too: Couture looks are often bigger and more complicated than ready-to-wear clothes (the collections shown in July are for the fall season, so bigger, more complicated and heavier).
But in Dior’s actual runway show, the collection felt anything but stifling. There was a similar sensibility in the Schiaparelli collection earlier in the day, but something about the air flow at the Musée Rodin put the lightness front and center. Chanel seemed equally concerned with comfort, with several looks featuring light, sheer tops. At Jean Paul Gaultier, some of the biggest couture-type creations ever sent down a runway were augmented with bare shoulders and bare backs.
See more Paris Couture Week looks from the runway and onlookers at the link in our bio. Visuals by @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
Pierpaolo Piccioli’s debut couture show for Balenciaga was a gorgeous parade of dramatic designs. The collection was recognized by many as a return to form, and while it was "beautiful, no question," as our chief fashion critic Vanessa Friedman wrote in her review, it also felt familiar.
Piccioli has embraced the extraordinary color sense that became his signature in his former job at Valentino, as well as the grace of form. He has combined that with classic Balenciaga shapes (the sack, the trapeze, the balloon skirt) made in an entirely contemporary way. On the surface, the designer is doing everything right. But is it right for right now?
Read @vvfriedman's full review of @balenciaga's Paris couture show at the link in our bio. Photos by @simbarashecha
posts.alt_text:
When most women think of Chanel ballet flats, they think of them in their prim and proper Rue Cambon element. The classic tan-and-black pair have a reputation as polished and Parisian or manicured and uptown.

But recently, a subset of creative, upwardly mobile women are traipsing around in the flats in flashy colors and funky materials. Often in physical jobs and on set for production or styling, these women wear the shoes with baggy cargos, blooming skirts or low-slung jeans, rather than the tweed skirt suits they may have been previously imagined with.

“They’re like a utility shoe,” Ariella Starkman, a creative strategist and producer in New York, said. “They add a level of polish when I’m doing the dirty work,” she added.

Unlike luxury bags, shoes often do not appreciate in value. But Chanel flats appear to be a different story. Tap the link in our bio to read more about how Chanel flats have become the shoe du jour for fashion girls. Photos by Getty Images

posts.caption:
Duran Lantink, the next-gen designer at Jean Paul Gaultier and the first permanent creative director to follow the brand’s founder, is a master provocateur.
It was Lantink, after all, who broke the internet last spring when he opened and closed a show for his own line with a man wearing a female chest prosthetic and a woman wearing a man’s. In October, he featured a bodysuit silk-screened with a naked male body, detailed genitalia and all, in his first Gaultier ready-to-wear show. Little wonder his Gaultier couture was the most-anticipated debut this week.
Nowadays, as fashion shows become more about driving engagement and name recognition, the more extreme and ridiculous a look, the better. And Lantink isn’t the only designer who seemingly strives for virality. Armani Privé, Robert Wun, Viktor and Rolf and Schiaparelli all made their bids for the attention economy. But some gimmicks work better than others. Tap the link in our bio to read @vvfriedman's analysis of the couture shows in Paris. Photos by @simbarashecha, Jean Paul Gaultier, Luca Tombolini, Umberto Fratini

posts.caption:
#VictoriaBeckham truly is the defining #WAG. She created such a distinct aesthetic that it is still referenced today. Her husband may have been captain but she famously never wore an England #football shirt. Instead opting for slogan tank tops paired with tiny shorts or low-rise jeans, oversized sunglasses, Louboutins and a Birkin.
She knew exactly who she was and rather than dressing like a typical football fan, she dressed like Victoria Beckham: international pop star and an emerging fashion figure. 20 years later and while her style is much more refined, we will never forget the OG wag.
posts.alt_text:
The #MiddleEast has produced some of the world’s greatest designers, but why do so many come from #Lebanon? Sat at the crossroads of Silk Road trade routes linking Asia, Europe, and Africa, Lebanon developed a tradition of textiles and embroidery, with skills passed down through generations.

Couture is a huge market In the Middle East due to the social culture of weddings and events. While Haute Couture is an institutional system in Paris,  the concept has existed for centuries across India, Pakistan, the Middle East, and China. In the West, couture works as a prestige marketing tool, elsewhere this level of craftsmanship has always been part of how people dress.

Unlike heritage brands which became corporatised and part of conglomerates, Lebanon’s couture boom produced independent designers like #ZuhairMurad, #ElieSaab, and #GeorgesHobeika, who still lead their own houses. Their success built on craftsmanship, personal reputation, and loyal private clients.

posts.caption:
#OliviaDean’s stylist truly loves her!! and when it came to dressing her biggest tour yet, @simonebeyene worked with some of the best designers and ateliers to reinterpret their collections into custom looks, capturing Olivia’s stage presence, movement, and personal style.
Some of Simone’s favourite looks include the custom Métiers d’Art 2026 #Chanel skirt (slide 5) and the Spring 2026 tasselled #Alaïa dress (slide 2) a fitting farewell to #PieterMulier’s tenure at the house.
The tour saw beautiful couture gowns take centre stage while preserving the warmth and intimacy that have become central to @oliviadeano’s artistic identity. Simone proves that thoughtful styling can be just as powerful a storytelling tool as the music itself.
Images by @lolamansell

posts.caption:
For decades, #Chanel couture shows have ended with a bridal look, but this time #MatthieuBlazy reimagined the traditional narrative as a story of independence. Matthieu was inspired by a book of fairy tales and #GabrielleChanel’s own story, from hardship to success. A wedding dress wasn’t Chanel’s fairytale because she never got married, so instead he closed with the “revenge dress.”
A tribute to Chanel’s life, which was defined by ambition, freedom, and creativity rather than marriage, while famously evoking the spirit of #PrincessDiana, a symbol of confidence and reclaiming one’s own narrative.

posts.caption:
#Givenchy Spring 1997 was #AlexanderMcqueens first collection after taking over from #JohnGalliano. It actually received mixed reactions, many felt McQueen was too young and too rebellious for a house built on traditional French elegance.
His appointment represented a huge shift in fashion, as younger designers began taking control of historic couture houses. McQueen himself later criticized the collection, but over time it became one of the most important moments in his career.
#Zendaya in @givenchy Spring 1997 and accompanying @philiptreacy headpiece, styled by @luxurylaw

posts.caption:
Inside the former chapel of a stately Parisian home lies #RogerVivier’s extraordinary archive. The climate-controlled “laboratory” houses more than 50 years of work by one of the world’s most influential shoe designers.
@rogervivier transformed footwear, pioneering the stiletto heel and the curved “Choc” heel. His work for  #ChristianDior gave shoes the same level of artistry and craftsmanship as Haute Couture. Decades later, his influence continues to shape contemporary fashion, with echoes of his designs still visible at houses including #Dior, #Prada, and #SaintLaurent

Justin Bieber for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
This is a real piece of history for us all. The past 10 years of lessons and learning have led up to this moment and I couldn’t be happier to share it with you all.
Some of you may be new followers who discovered us last week and some of you may have been here since day 1, either way this is for everyone who aligns with what it means to be an outlander.
Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.
Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com
PRINT ISN’T DEAD.
🫂 @lilbieber
📸 @sweetcian
🔗 @freenjoy

Naomi Osaka for Outlander Magazine wearing her custom Nike Roland Garros looks designed by Nike & Kevin Germanier!

Roland Garros’ best dressed just raised the bar again. Naomi’s custom Nike looks were designed by Kevin Germanier, the couture designer turning upcycled materials into something iconic. Clay court season needed a moment like this, so we took her to an authentic orange clay court in paris to match the energy she’ll be surrounded by this week😤🤝

Travis Scott for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
GANG, TRAVIS SCOTT IN HERMÈS FOR OUR FIRST EVER MAGAZINE COVER. THIS IS UNHEARD OF, AND BETTER YET? IT WAS FOR OUTLANDER MAGAZINE. I AM HUMBLED.
WOW, what a moment. My only wish in this life is that what I’ve spent the last 10 years building acts as a strong form of inspiration for your own creative careers - I came from no fashion background, I had no connections & I had no idea what I was doing, but, I did it anyway.
If you didn’t know by now, my name is @callum.mccaff , I founded Outlander - and I’m here to remind everyone that PRINT ISN’T DEAD.

posts.caption:
India's Renee Noronha has won first place in the 18-24 category at the Ironman Philippines. Completing the gruelling challenge at 15:55:37, the 20-year-old IIT Madras student upheld held her record of being the youngest Indian female Ironman finisher.
Renee battled torrential rains and unforgiving temperatures to complete the race. In conversation with SheThePeople, the triathlete also revealed that she participated on day 3 of her period, making the victory a profound reminder of her strength and resilience as a woman.
With this win, Renee has now qualified for the Ironman World Championship Hawaii, scheduled for October 2026. She is also on a mission to set a new Guinness world record to become the youngest person in the world to do a full Ironman on 6 continents. She is on track to achieve this by April 2027, at the age of 21.
Read full interview at www.shethepeople.tv. | @renee.noronha
#Ironman #Philippines #sports full Ironman challenge, women in sports, women in endurance sports, mumbai sports, Chennai sports, IIT Madras student, women in STEM, ironman Kona, triathlon, marathon, menstrual awareness

posts.caption:
A growing number of ladies are making the first move, changing the rules of dating. A few months ago, I ran into a newly divorced friend sulking in the corner of a party. Hugs and tears followed until she asked me the all-important question: how do I start again?
My friend had a rather genuine problem; she was married to a national celebrity, and her divorce was rather public too. Since her ex was a famous figure, she discovered she had no suitors thanks to being in the public eye herself. “It’s been a long and lonely year. And no one has even shown interest,” she admitted.
“Don’t wait to be asked out, you make the first move,” I replied. She was aghast; she made a face as if I had asked her to get her tongue pierced. She said she had never asked a man out. “Do it once, cross that bridge, and you’ll find how easy and empowering it is,” I smiled.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full opinion piece by @namratazakaria
#dating #love dating advice, relationships, dating apps, couple reels, first move, bollywood, Deepika Padukone, Queen, A suitable boy, empowerment, feminism

posts.caption:
A college girl from Kolkata decided to take her first steps in the entertainment industry, albeit being besieged by loads of ‘what ifs’. What if she doesn’t get projects? What if her introverted behaviour prevents her from acting? Putting all these questions aside, she nursed her passion for being an actor.
That’s Tillotama Shome, an actor recognised for winning the trust of people, a storyteller who doesn’t hesitate in refusing scripts she doesn’t align with.
Born to parents who taught her the understanding of affection and freedom, she always aimed to be financially capable. She began her career with the film ‘Monsoon Wedding’ and built a career defined by versatility rather than convention.
Her feature film Boksho Bondi, also produced by her, was recently awarded by FIPRESCI (The International Federation of Film Critics).
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full interview by @_shivanilamba
#tillotamashome #bollywood #cinema Boksho Bondi, actress, body objectification, storytelling, film characters, entertainment industry, trending viral, Monsoon Wedding, Qissa, Acting

posts.caption:
ThumpN, founded by Varun Khare, has raised $3.75M in a pre‑seed round with Arijit Singh(@arijitsingh), Sunidhi Chauhan (@sunidhichauhan5), Badshah (@badboyshah), and Paytm’s Vijay Shekhar Sharma backing it.
It calls itself India’s first AI‑native live entertainment discovery and ticketing service. At the center is Shadow, an AI agent that personalises recommendations and works with curators, artists, promoters, venue insiders, to surface shows people might otherwise miss.
The promise is scale, polish, and accessibility for a fragmented industry. Live entertainment gets treated like a serious economy, with celebrity capital and professional infrastructure behind it. But the risk is cultural flattening.
AI has already crept into how music is being made; maybe its rightful place is on the curation side, helping audiences find events they’d skip otherwise. Even then, smaller grassroots gigs can slip through the cracks if the system privileges what’s mainstream and measurable.
posts.alt_text:
Young Indians chased imported superfoods, only to find their grandparents had been fermenting rice all along. Pakhala, panta bhat, pazhaya soru—these aren’t just breakfasts, they’re identity markers.

Cooling in summer, probiotic by nature, and tied to rituals from temples to harvest festivals, fermented rice is returning to menus as both wellness food and cultural statement.

Full story at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang) 

#fermentedrice #Indianheritage #foodculture #guthealth #sustainableliving

posts.caption:
For anyone who has lived in Mumbai long enough, Kala Ghoda is a map of memories: the arts festival that began in 1999, dates at Jehangir Art Gallery, meals at Trishna and Kala Ghoda Café, and, more recently, Americano and Nicobar.
Over the past decade, this once densely mixed cultural and commercial quarter has filled with designer boutiques, converting former lawyers’ offices into retail stores.
This week, Maharashtra Chief Minister Devendra Fadnavis inaugurated the Kala Ghoda Art Avenue, a BMC initiative modelled on pedestrian districts such as London’s Carnaby Street and Glasgow’s Buchanan Street.
Its first phase transforms five lanes with cobbled pathways, murals, public seating, and designated art zones. On weekends vehicle access will be limited.
Future phases propose extending the pedestrian precinct across more lanes, introducing regulated al fresco dining, and creating a public plaza around Kala Ghoda’s sculpture of the riderless horse.
Yet Kala Ghoda was always made for pedestrians. Its newest chapter feels like a return to its original rhythm rather than a reinvention: walking, wandering, and lingering.
#KalaGhoda #MumbaiCulture #PedestrianDistrict #CityDesign #UrbanShift

posts.caption: Yes so true. In Gujarat nature clubs for children exist in only two cities. There are more adventure camps then nature appreciation camps. In ahmedabad there is none. The district has so many different ecosystems - ponds, rivers, scrub forests and riparian green belts - all being destroyed in the name of development. Ahmedabad is full of builders, architects, designers abd planners - and they have ruined the soul of the city. Even if they care they will not speak out bcoz their next project will get stalled. It’s the city with the maximum number of old trees and scrub forests being erased per month and the maximum number of Miyawaki scams green painted by construction companies and also the maximum number of new buildings with empty apartments. We have studied the flora between Gandhinagar - viranmgan - Shela - Shihabag and along large ravines of the river - what still gets Sabarmati water and the diversity is mind blowing and gorgeous. But no one cares. We rewilded our land which everyone should be doing but people here care more about the increasing resale value of land rather than sustaining ecology and biodiversity. And the sad part is that the urban sickness of being uncaring towards nature is now reaching the villages as the cities expand. We are like a cancer.

posts.caption:
In India, antibiotics are often treated like everyday pills: easy to buy, easy to share, easy to take when rest feels inconvenient.
Limited sick leave, rushed consultations, time poverty, and over-the-counter access have made self-medication dangerously normal.
The result is antimicrobial resistance, where medicines stop working against infections they once treated. When antibiotics fail, routine illnesses become harder to cure and modern medicine loses one of its most essential safeguards.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#health #antimicrobialresistance #India #medicalcrisis #pillculture

posts.caption:
Delhi is not a market Lululemon (@lululemon) needs to explain itself to. It has long been understood, just not officially stocked.
The Canadian brand’s first India store will open at DLF Promenade later this year through its franchise partnership with Tata CLiQ (@tatacliqfashion), converting years of airport hauls and global wardrobe fluency into local retail.
The sharper play is distribution: the Delhi store arrives alongside launches on Tata CLiQ Luxury and Tata CLiQ Fashion, making the debut physical in the capital but national online. The store will carry Lululemon’s technical apparel and lifestyle pieces across yoga, Pilates, running, training, tennis, golf, and everyday movement.

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

Jonathan Anderson just showed his first couture collection for Dior. If you do not understand why it mattered, this post is for you.

John Galliano was front row. The artisans were the opening act. The silhouettes came from ceramicist Magdalene Odundo.

If you are in Paris: the show space at Musée Rodin opens to the public from 28 January for one week. The new couture collection, archival Dior pieces, and the Odundo ceramics that inspired it. You can see it yourself.

This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.

posts.caption:
Talent alone won’t build your career. How you handle yourself will.
I’ve watched incredibly talented stylists stall out at assistant level while less talented ones build sustainable careers. The difference isn’t skill. It’s how they approached the business side of fashion.
Fashion moves fast. Make the wrong moves early and those mistakes follow you around. Burn the wrong bridge, act entitled at the wrong moment, undercharge consistently, ignore contracts - these things compound. Before you know it, you’re working twice as hard as everyone else for half the opportunities.
Some stylists never recover from early career mistakes because nobody told them what not to do. They assumed talent would be enough. It’s not.
The stylists who last aren’t always the most creative. They’re the ones who understood that professionalism, boundaries, business strategy, and resilience matter just as much as aesthetic vision.
This carousel breaks down the mistakes that ruin styling careers. Not to scare you, but to stop you making them in the first place.
Swipe through. Take notes. Don’t be the stylist who learns these lessons the hard way.
Want the courses, templates, and community to build your career properly? The Studio of Stylist Elixir gives you the foundation so many stylists spend years figuring out alone.
Comment STUDIO below or head to link in bio 🔗🔗

posts.caption:
Here are the things people do not say out loud about this industry.
You are expected to just know. How to set a rate, how to pitch, how to build a book that gets you hired. There is no open handbook, so most of us start in silence, hoping a door opens, too afraid to ask because we feel we should already understand it all. Then come the rates no one prepared you for, and the invoices you chase for months with no clear contract.
None of that is your fault. It is a gap in how the industry passes on knowledge, not a gap in your talent. And it is changing. You can learn this part properly, price with confidence, and present your work like the professional you already are.
That is the whole reason the Studio exists. Rates, pitching, portfolios and getting booked, sorted together with 100+ working stylists, no gatekeeping. Comment STUDIO and I’ll send you in free for a week 💚 The price more than doubles in August, so joining now locks it.

posts.caption:
I used to think I was bad at networking.
I’d see other stylists getting opportunities, building connections, landing collaborations, and I’d wonder what I was missing. Why did it seem so easy for them? Why did every industry event feel awkward? Why did my DMs go unanswered?
Turns out, I wasn’t bad at it. I just didn’t know the system. Networking in fashion isn’t about being the loudest person in the room or knowing all the right people from day one. It’s not about having perfect confidence or saying the perfect thing every time.
The stylists who are consistently getting work, building real relationships, and opening doors? They’ve figured out where to show up, what to say, and how to build connections that actually matter. And once you know that, everything changes.
This carousel walks you through some of the truths about networking that I wish someone had told me years ago. But if you want the full system (the actual outreach templates, the step-by-step frameworks, the real strategies that work) that’s what we teach inside The Studio.
We break down the parts of building a career in fashion that no one else talks about. The messy bits.
The « I don’t know what to say » moments.
The « how do I actually get work? » questions.
And you’re surrounded by a community of stylists from around the world who are doing this with you.
Comment STUDIO and I’ll send you the link to join 💚
posts.alt_text:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.

It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.

And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.

Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.

Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.

That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.

Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption:
Your portfolio is either opening doors or closing them.
Most stylists fill theirs with everything they’ve ever done - every test shoot, every behind-the-scenes moment, every job crammed in like a scrapbook. They think it shows range. It doesn’t. It shows you don’t know what you’re best at.
Clients aren’t looking at your portfolio to see your journey. They’re looking for one thing: proof you can deliver what they need. If they have to dig through clutter to find it, they won’t. They’ll just move on.
A strong portfolio is curated, not cluttered. Clear credits. Simple layout. Work that shows exactly what you want to be booked for - nothing else.
These are the red flags I see over and over again. Swipe through and be honest with yourself about how many are in yours.
Comment “STUDIO” to try the Studio free for 30 days. Inside you get portfolio templates, guides, a full portfolio workshop, and monthly calls where I review your work directly.

My jaw is on the floor. For the New York premiere of The Odyssey, Zendaya wore the ethereal “Ange” gown from avant-garde label Matières Fécales’ debut Haute Couture collection, paired with Chopard jewels, stunning hairstyling, and a no-makeup makeup look I can already see becoming a trend. These two are so ahead of the game it’s crazy…

posts.caption:
It is almost a cliché at this point for fashion people to fall victim to the art world. The crossover is everywhere: runway set designs, museum and gallery retrospectives, conceptual collections, and couture that doubles as sculpture. Even the 2026 Met Gala explicitly bridged the gap by focusing on art and the human body.
For me, the real obsession started this past May when Celine partnered with Sotheby’s for their New York marquee auctions, anchored by the masterworks of Mark Rothko. And since, I’ve gone from appreciating art on a surface level to becoming quietly obsessed. And over the last few months, I’ve curated a list of my favorites. Here are four of them.
Read the latest on Substack: The Citizen’s Poste | posteposteposte.com
#Substack #Rothko #Celine #Sothebys

posts.caption:
The fashion-tech world has found itself in the middle of a major controversy. A recent Bloomberg investigation alleged that Phia, the AI shopping app co-founded by Phoebe Gates and Sophia Kianni, has been improperly taking credit for online sales it did not generate. According to the report, the app’s browser extension injected its own affiliate referral codes during checkout—a practice known as “cookie stuffing”—potentially redirecting commissions away from the publishers, creators, and influencers who originally drove the purchase.
Phia has strongly disputed any intentional wrongdoing, stating the issue was the result of a temporary coding bug that was fixed immediately after it was identified.
Regardless of intent, the story has sparked an industry-wide conversation about trust, attribution, and accountability in the rapidly growing world of AI-powered shopping tools in a world where transparency is becoming more essential and less accessible than ever before.
What are your thoughts?
#FashionTech #Phia

Working alongside longtime stylist Law Roach, her wardrobe for The Odyssey has embraced true method dressing, leaning into Grecian drapery, sculptural corsetry, and silhouettes that feel worthy of the epic itself.
From custom Jacquemus to archival Givenchy and Schiaparelli couture literally straight off the runway, every look has balanced cinematic storytelling with impeccable craftsmanship.
At this point, it’s hard to think of anyone executing modern method dressing better than Zendaya.

posts.caption:
A little over a year into Jonathan Anderson’s Dior, and the verdict is… split. On one hand, we’re getting some collections that feel studied, referential, and intent on reshaping what Dior means now. On the other, a growing sense that something is missing: the fantasy, the excess, the unmistakable “Dior Myth” that once made the house feel untouchable.
The question is where does Dior go from here—deeper into fantasy, or back toward tradition? Drop your thoughts.
#Dior #JonathanAnderson

after last year’s collaboration, Timberland and Jacquemus are back with a fresh take on the iconic 3-Eye Lug Boat Shoe.
this time, the classic silhouette arrives in a bold Lagoon Blue, a colour lifted straight from Jacquemus’ spring/summer 2027 collection Le Bonheur. crafted from premium full-grain leather with Timberland’s signature lug sole, the shoe is finished with tonal branding, matching laces and subtle Jacquemus metal hardware.
part preppy classic, part fashion statement… this might just be the boat shoe of the summer.

after hosting an intimate $10 pop-up show in Brooklyn last week, Charli did it all over again in London, packing out another tiny venue with fans, unreleased tracks from “Music, Fashion, Film” and surprise appearances from The Femcels, Jane Remover and Thy Slaughter.
it’s the kind of rollout only Charli could pull off: small rooms, huge hype and fans who know they’ll probably never experience the same show twice.

posts.caption:
this one’s about Vetements’ shoes experiments.
from the now-iconic lighter heels to heels made from coins, exaggerated pointed toes, towering thigh-high boots, flame platforms, cowboy boots and impossible silhouettes, the brand spent years asking one question: how weird can footwear get?
every season felt like another challenge to everything we thought a shoe was supposed to look like, and somehow, they almost always worked. whether you loved them or hated them, one thing was certain: you couldn’t ignore them.
probably no one has had more fun with footwear than Vetements

posts.caption:
what looks like buttery leather is actually “Silk”: Louis Vuitton’s brand-new material innovation first unveiled on Pharrell Williams’ fall/winter 2026 runway.
woven from silk and reclaimed nylon, it’s water-repellent, crease-resistant, incredibly lightweight and designed to feel as luxurious as it looks. inspired by the strength of vintage silk parachutes, it even has the durability to support serious weight (yes, the “Silk Tech City”bag can carry up to 50kg).
now available worldwide across ready-to-wear, bags, luggage, footwear and accessories, “Silk Tech” feels less like a seasonal fabric launch and more like the beginning of Louis Vuitton’s next signature material.
#TFO #louisvuitton #pharell #silktech

𝐋𝐮𝐩𝐢𝐭𝐚 𝐍𝐲𝐨𝐧𝐠’𝐨 𝐖𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐬 𝐒𝐚𝐛𝐲𝐚𝐬𝐚𝐜𝐡𝐢 𝐇𝐢𝐠𝐡 𝐉𝐞𝐰𝐞𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐫𝐲
Her casting as Helen of Troy in Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey may have sparked significant online backlash and debate about historical accuracy and Homer’s original descriptions, but actor Lupita Nyong’o is living up to the adage of the “face that launched a thousand ships”. For the New York premiere of the multi-starrer, the actor wore a gold lace Nicholas Oakwell Couture corset gown with a Sabyasachi High Jewellery choker necklace crafted in gold with pearls and diamonds and a tiger centre-piece. Paired with an 18-karat gold Bengal Tiger diamond ring, she looked ready to silence armies and win the style war.
𝐕𝐢𝐤𝐭𝐨𝐫&𝐑𝐨𝐥𝐟’𝐬 𝐒𝐨𝐥𝐨 𝐄𝐱𝐡𝐢𝐛𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐌𝐢𝐥𝐚𝐧
Ahead of its retrospective exhibition slated to debut in Paris next year, Dutch couture house Viktor&Rolf will host its first-ever solo show this September at concept retail and gallery space 10 Corso Como, as part of the store’s 35th anniversary celebrations. Titled Spectrum, the show has been put together by curator and critic Alessio de Navasques and spans designers Viktor Horsting and Rolf Snoeren’s three-decade-long journey in design. On display will be 20 haute couture looks, shown in pairs in a bid to underline the duo’s philosophy of contrasts and duality.
𝐎𝐥𝐢𝐯𝐢𝐞𝐫 𝐑𝐨𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐓𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐑𝐞𝐢𝐧𝐬 𝐚𝐭 𝐑𝐚𝐛𝐚𝐧𝐧𝐞
French luxury house Rabanne has appointed Olivier Rousteing as its new creative director. The designer who spent 14 years at Balmain, succeeds Julien Dossena, whose departure was announced earlier this year. Rousteing will present his debut collection for Rabanne at Paris Fashion Week in March 2027.

posts.caption:
Even if you know nothing about Masaba Gupta (@masabagupta), at the very least you know that she’s famous. Some people know her simply as Masaba, founder of the fashion label House of Masaba (@houseofmasaba) that, for the last 17 years, has been making playfully printed contemporary Indian clothing. Others know her as the star of the Netflix show ‘Masaba Masaba’. To the chronically online, she’s the powerful influencer, the person brands go to when they want a cool, articulate personality to represent them. The makeup-obsessed know her via LoveChild by Masaba (@lovechildbymasaba), the beauty brand she started in 2021, which has taken her obsession with makeup and turned it into products built specifically around Indian skin tones.
Yet, as she continues to do it all, there is a change underway. As we walk out of her office, Masaba casually drops a piece of news. “I’m moving away from ready-to-wear entirely. I think spring/summer 2026 is the last one we’ll do. The focus is going to be on occasion and bridalwear.” It’s a pretty big declaration, and a timely one, considering that later this month Masaba will be a part of the FDCI Hyundai India Couture Week in New Delhi. Right now, it feels like Masaba is moving into a new era, one where she’s more of a founder and businesswoman than a niche fashion designer.
At the link in bio, @butoolbegum spends the day in the company of the enterprising designer, business owner, and new mom.

posts.caption:
Fatherhood is a new role for the actor who first got noticed for his performance in ‘Masaan’ (2015). In fact, it seems this off-camera Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09) is perhaps closer to Deepak Chaudhary, the character he played in the film. A sensitive, ambitious young man coming to terms with the world around him. That character, Kaushal says, changed him. But not for the obvious reasons.
“Until then success, to me, was where you reached [in life]. It was never about where you started from,” he explains. “That character, starting from the crematorium ghat in Benaras and going on to work as an engineer in the Railways in Allahabad [now Prayagraj]... He had a far bigger life arc than I’ve covered in my life. Till today,” he says, tapping the arm rest for emphasis. “After that I started having so much empathy for those around me, especially those from far less privileged backgrounds, because I don’t know where they have started from.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
You have probably bumped into an indie-bookstore-branded tote-bag-wearing, iced-matcha-sipping, Joan Didion-reading, Laufey-loving performative male. Maybe more than once. It’s the type that’s everywhere today. It’s a type that perfectly fits 'The Catcher in the Rye'’s Holden Caulfield’s definition of ‘phony’, if the JD Salinger classic was based in 2026.
We’ll be honest. Though we had heard of this classic 1951 novel, it never made it to our TBR list in the exploration of coming-of-age literature; Little Women and The Bell Jar still sit on top of our book pile, though. That was until last week, when we took it on as an assignment.
At the link in bio, our Gen Z interns @ara_mishra and @nandini_sanghvi take on Caulfield on the 75th anniversary of 'The Catcher in the Rye'.

posts.caption:
“It is a Friday afternoon when Mumbai’s Phoenix Palladium, which only recently recovered from the carnage of the Zara summer sale, hosts a horde of a different kind—the fans, guests, and press assembled for The Odyssey’s (@theodysseymovie) India premiere.
I arrive 15 minutes early, only to be informed of a 30-minute delay. It’s fitting that Nolan’s first India premiere would be happening in both senses of the term “Indian Standard Time”. Some Nolan fans have a lot to say about the delay. “I want to speak to your supervisor”, in particular, sticks out on its third repetition. Odd, I thought: Nolan fans should have a particular gift for understanding the manipulation of time.
Since I am about to watch a Nolan film—notorious for grand music and whispered dialogue—I prime my ears to listen extra closely, a fringe benefit of which turns out to be some excellent eavesdropping.”
At the link in bio, @saniyajafferrr gives us a peek inside the premiere that revealed there is Barbenheimer everywhere for those with eyes to see.

posts.caption:
Benny Prasad (@bennyprasad) is a gospel musician who’s also credited with inventing a “bentar”, a cross between a bongo and a guitar. His claim to fame, however, is something else entirely—he holds the record for being the fastest man to travel 245 countries. It’s not a recent feat; that distinction came back in 2010. Last month, however, a video of him nonchalantly flipping through his stack of 16 Indian passports gained 10 million views within 24 hours of posting.
It’s a bizarre twist for the 50-year-old who, at 19, had suffered a personal crisis stemming from severe illness and academic failure. It was then that he turned to his faith, picked up the guitar, and began touring as a musician. By 2002, Prasad decided that he wanted to take his music around the world, so he set a goal to tour all nations by 2010. In 2006 he entered the Limca Book of Records for being the “most travelled Indian musician”. Soon, he set the other, more enviable record: the fastest man to visit all 245 nations (194 sovereign states, 51 dependent territories, and even Antarctica) in six years, six months, and 22 days.
But honestly? The record isn’t even the wildest part. At the link in bio, @sirf.anushka chats with Prasad about why he's anyone but your average travel influencer.
